messages.content:
You paraphrase a research PROBLEM STATEMENT across 7 ML domains.
Your output is a *domain-grounded problem description* — written in
the target domain's natural research vocabulary, but stating only
the failure mode, NOT a proposed solution.

## Two-step procedure
1. Extract the FAILURE MODE from the input query: the underlying
   gap, bottleneck, or constraint. Strip out any solution direction
   the query may already suggest. Express the failure mode as one
   abstract sentence (the "essence").
2. For each domain, describe an analogous failure mode in that
   domain — using *3-5 domain-specific technical terms* so the
   paraphrase reads like a problem statement an actual researcher
   in that domain would write in their paper's introduction.

## Strict rules — follow exactly
- Use PROBLEM vocabulary: "X is slow when ...", "Y fails when ...",
  "Z is costly because ...", "W is unavailable but needed for ..."
- DO NOT use SOLUTION vocabulary: NO "use ... to ...", NO "build ...
  that ...", NO "apply ... for ...", NO "design a ... which ...".
- DO NOT name a specific algorithmic *solution* (e.g. "two-stage
  cascade", "Gumbel-Softmax sampling"). The paraphrase describes a
  problem; the analogous mechanisms will be discovered downstream.
- DO use domain-specific *concepts and quantities* that bound the
  failure mode (e.g. "candidate pool", "feature map resolution",
  "rollout horizon", "phoneme alignment") — these anchor the
  problem in the domain's natural research vocabulary.
- The paraphrase MUST end as a problem statement, not a solution
  prescription.

## Domain vocabulary anchors (use 3-5 per paraphrase, NOT exhaustive)
  LLM_NLP    : token, decoding step, KV cache, context length,
               attention head, perplexity, in-context examples
  MULTIMODAL : visual token, image embedding, cross-modal alignment,
               query-conditioned attention, caption, VQA accuracy
  CV         : feature map, patch token, spatial resolution,
               receptive field, anchor box, segmentation mask
  RL         : policy, value function, rollout, reward signal,
               trajectory, exploration, sample efficiency
  IR_REC     : candidate pool, query-document score, reranking,
               cross-encoder, recall@k, embedding index
  SPEECH     : acoustic frame, phoneme, spectrogram, alignment,
               WER, streaming latency, vocoder
  ROBOTICS   : sensorimotor loop, manipulation policy, dynamics
               model, motion plan, sim-to-real, contact reasoning

Push hard — even cross-domain mappings that *feel* forced often
surface useful seeds when essence-aligned. Only emit `null` for a
domain when the failure mode genuinely has no analog there.

## Reference example
  Input query: "Sequential LLM decoding is slow because each token
                needs a full large-model forward pass; how can a small
                draft model propose tokens in parallel that the large
                model verifies without quality loss?"

  GOOD essence: "Sequential single-step inference incurs O(N)
                 expensive forward passes when N items must be
                 decided in order, even when many decisions could
                 be made in parallel."

  GOOD IR_REC paraphrase: "In information retrieval, cross-encoder
                            reranking of a candidate pool requires a
                            forward pass per (query, document) pair;
                            recall@k depends on this expensive
                            scoring step yet most candidates could
                            be filtered with cheaper signals before
                            final ranking."
                            ↑ uses: cross-encoder, candidate pool,
                                     query-document scoring, recall@k,
                                     reranking — IR vocabulary

  GOOD CV paraphrase:      "In dense visual perception, per-patch
                            token processing through a heavy encoder
                            dominates inference latency for high
                            spatial resolution inputs; segmentation
                            quality depends on this dense scoring
                            even though most patches are background
                            or low-saliency."
                            ↑ uses: patch token, spatial resolution,
                                     segmentation, dense scoring

  BAD IR_REC paraphrase (DO NOT produce — solution-form):
                          "Build a two-stage ranker where a cheap
                           encoder proposes candidates and a
                           cross-encoder verifies accepted spans."

  BAD IR_REC paraphrase (DO NOT produce — too abstract):
                          "Relevance estimation is sequential and
                           costly when each item must be scored in
                           turn; quality must not degrade under
                           cheaper alternatives."
                           ↑ no IR-specific vocabulary, would
                             match poorly against real IR seeds

Always produce the GOOD form: domain-vocabulary-rich problem
statement.

Output JSON:
  {
    "essence":  "<one sentence stating the abstract failure mode>",
    "paraphrases": {
      "LLM_NLP":    "<domain-grounded problem statement>",
      "MULTIMODAL": "...",
      "CV":         "...",
      "RL":         "...",
      "IR_REC":     "...",
      "SPEECH":     "...",
      "ROBOTICS":   null
    }
  }

Query: Test-time distributions diverge from training data along axes
the model never saw; how can we make models robust to such
shifts without observing the target distribution at training?

You synthesize a method that addresses the user's query by drawing on
a set of retrieved research seeds. Each seed is a published
contribution with a (problem, method, domain, paper_title) tuple, and
it arrives with a "Retrieved via" lens — the text (raw query or a
paraphrase reframing the query for a specific domain) that surfaced
this seed during retrieval. Use the lens as a hint about WHY this
seed was considered; the paraphrase lens often reveals an analogy
between the query and a mechanism family the raw query alone would
not have surfaced. The seeds may come from any subset of ML domains;
use those whose mechanisms genuinely help, and do not force
connections to seeds that do not fit.

Distractor handling: when a seed's lens explicitly indicates it was
NOT retrieved for this query (e.g., "uniform random sample of the
library; no targeted retrieval"), treat that seed as a distractor.
Ignore it unless its mechanism genuinely fits the query for reasons
unrelated to retrieval. It is correct and expected to leave such
seeds out of inspired_by; do not pad inspired_by to make it look
like the distractors were used.

Your output MUST be JSON:
  {
    "method":    "<3–5 paragraphs describing the proposed method end-to-end>",
    "rationale": "<why this addresses the user query, in 1–3 paragraphs>",
    "inspired_by": [
      { "seed_id": "<from one of the provided seeds>",
        "domain":  "<that seed's domain>",
        "borrowed_aspect": "<short noun phrase: what was taken from this seed>" },
      ...
    ]
  }

Provenance: list a seed in inspired_by when its mechanism genuinely
contributed to the method. You MAY include steps that no seed
supports when they are necessary glue logic, problem-specific
adaptation, or a novel addition; do not omit a needed step just
because no seed inspired it. Do not pad inspired_by with seeds that
did not actually contribute.

## Query
Test-time distributions diverge from training data along axes
the model never saw; how can we make models robust to such
shifts without observing the target distribution at training?


## Retrieved seeds

### Seed baa2fa7d385b [IR_REC] — "Less Is Better: Unweighted Data Subsampling via Influence Function"
- Retrieved via: Test-time distributions diverge from training data along axes
the model never saw; how can we make models robust to such
shifts without observing the target distribution at training?

- Problem: Influence-based deterministic ‘dropout’ sampling can become over-confident on a specific validation distribution, hurting out-of-sample generalization under distribution shift.
- Method:  Develop probabilistic sampling functions with bounded gradients to control confidence and improve robustness across distributions within a χ²-divergence ball around the empirical distribution. Theoretically, they prove the worst-case risk over this uncertainty set is Lipschitz in the influence vector with constant scaling as O(σ√((2δ+1)/n)), where σ bounds the sampling function’s gradient (Theorem 3), highlighting instability of deterministic dropout (unbounded gradient). They also introduce a surrogate confidence metric Γ(φ)=‖θ_ε−θ‖² and show it shares the same σ-dependent change rate (Theorem 4), and instantiate practical Linear and Sigmoid sampling functions that tune σ via a single parameter.

### Seed 1437553f99a7 [LLM_NLP] — "On Training Data Influence of GPT Models"
- Retrieved via: Test-time distributions diverge from training data along axes
the model never saw; how can we make models robust to such
shifts without observing the target distribution at training?

- Problem: Training data attribution (TDA) for autoregressive GPT models has focused mostly on test loss and often ties simulator parameters to seen training indices, limiting generalization to unseen data and overlooking richer training dynamics.
- Method:  GPTfluence introduces a featurized simulator that models training as an n-th order Markov process with multiplicative and additive influence factors. It computes per-batch effects via α and β, which are aggregated from per-example factors A_i,j and B_i derived from pairwise interactions between frozen pre-trained text encodings of training and test examples. Using a pre-trained encoder (e.g., BERT/GPT) for both training and test inputs enables parameter sharing over features rather than indices, allowing autoregressive prediction of full performance trajectories and robust generalization to unseen training/test data.

### Seed 66876933302f [IR_REC] — "ReLoop2: Building Self-Adaptive Recommendation Models via Responsive Error Compensation Loop"
- Retrieved via: Test-time distributions diverge from training data along axes
the model never saw; how can we make models robust to such
shifts without observing the target distribution at training?

- Problem: Online recommender systems face non-stationary data and test-time distribution shift, while gradient-based incremental updates adapt too slowly for large CTR models.
- Method:  ReLoop2 introduces a slow–fast learning paradigm with a training-free, non-parametric error memory that stores recent error samples (hidden state, base prediction, and ground-truth) observed during serving. At inference, it retrieves similar samples to the current hidden representation and uses attention with a temperature to estimate the prediction error; this error is then used to compensate the base model’s output with a tunable weight and clamping to [0,1]. The approach continually refreshes the memory online, creating a self-correcting loop for fast test-time adaptation, and is model-agnostic and orthogonal to incremental training.

### Seed d54cefc9ff77 [MULTIMODAL] — "Plug-and-Play VQA: Zero-shot VQA by Conjoining Large Pretrained Models with Zero Training"
- Retrieved via: Text–image models show degraded cross-modal alignment and reduced VQA accuracy when visual tokens and image embeddings at test time arise from styles or object compositions absent from training captions, and paired target-domain data is unavailable during training.
- Problem: Zero-shot VQA needs to reason over images and questions without any additional multimodal training; generic, image-wide captions often miss question-relevant details, limiting a text-only QA model’s effectiveness.
- Method:  Use a pretrained VLM (BLIP) both to localize question-relevant regions and to generate diverse, question-guided captions. Relevance is computed by a GradCAM-style aggregation over BLIP’s cross-attention in the Image-grounded Text Encoder: multiply cross-attention scores by gradients of the image–text similarity and average over heads/tokens to get per-patch relevance, then sample K′ patches per caption proportionally to this relevance. From these patches, generate N diverse captions via stochastic top-k decoding (e.g., k=50) and drop near-duplicates. This produces captions with higher answer-hit rates and boosts zero-shot VQA accuracy, outperforming generic/random patch captioning and even 5 human captions when using 100 question-guided captions.

### Seed cd8f80037def [MULTIMODAL] — "Visual Semantic Reasoning for Image-Text Matching"
- Retrieved via: Text–image models show degraded cross-modal alignment and reduced VQA accuracy when visual tokens and image embeddings at test time arise from styles or object compositions absent from training captions, and paired target-domain data is unavailable during training.
- Problem: Pure metric learning may not sufficiently enforce that the learned image embeddings encode captionable semantics needed for robust cross-modal alignment.
- Method:  Jointly optimize a hinge-based triplet ranking loss with hard negatives in the shared embedding space (using simple inner-product similarity) together with an auxiliary sequence-to-sequence caption generation loss with attention on the same visual features. This multi-objective training encourages embeddings that are both discriminative for retrieval and predictive of captions, improving R@1 over training without the generation objective.

### Seed 37f118fcc30e [MULTIMODAL] — "DetCLIPv2: Scalable Open-Vocabulary Object Detection Pre-training via Word-Region Alignment"
- Retrieved via: Text–image models show degraded cross-modal alignment and reduced VQA accuracy when visual tokens and image embeddings at test time arise from styles or object compositions absent from training captions, and paired target-domain data is unavailable during training.
- Problem: Open-vocabulary detection needs to learn from massive web image–text pairs, but captions lack instance-level boxes, VL distillation suffers domain gaps, and pseudo-labeling limits accuracy and scalability.
- Method:  DetCLIPv2 performs end-to-end joint pre-training on detection, grounding, and raw image–text pairs under a unified formulation, learning fine-grained word–region alignment directly from captions. For each noun phrase, it matches the best region proposal via a max-similarity (1-to-1) set alignment and optimizes a text-to-image contrastive loss only, which mitigates the partial-label problem in web data. It selects proposals for contrast by scoring regions with the maximum similarity to all concepts in the local batch, focusing on text-relevant regions. Alternating data types during training creates a flywheel between localization and recognition, and using low-resolution inputs for web pairs enables efficient scaling (e.g., 13× more pairs with similar training time).

### Seed 46eddd57427e [CV] — "Robust Instance Segmentation through Reasoning about Multi-Object Occlusion"
- Retrieved via: Recognition and segmentation degrade when feature map statistics shift because patch token distributions, spatial resolution, or object scales at test time differ from training, causing anchor box priors and resulting segmentation masks to fail without target-domain samples.
- Problem: Per-object, per-pixel feed-forward segmentations often conflict in overlap regions (multiple objects claiming the same pixels), especially under heavy or same-category occlusions, leading to erroneous masks and classifications.
- Method:  Introduce an Occlusion Reasoning Module (ORM) that operates on the per-object likelihood maps. It detects conflicts where two objects both predict foreground, resolves them via pixel-level competition by assigning each contested pixel to the object with higher foreground likelihood, and then recovers the pairwise occlusion order by comparing the number of reassigned pixels in the conflict set. Using this order, it enforces consistent labeling in overlaps and feeds the corrected occlusion priors back into the CompositionalNet to mask occluded features and recompute class likelihoods, iterating this top-down self-correction to improve both segmentation and classification under occlusion.

### Seed eedb9fef0129 [CV] — "S2S2: Semantic Stacking for Robust Semantic Segmentation in Medical Imaging"
- Retrieved via: Recognition and segmentation degrade when feature map statistics shift because patch token distributions, spatial resolution, or object scales at test time differ from training, causing anchor box priors and resulting segmentation masks to fail without target-domain samples.
- Problem: Medical image segmentation models trained on scarce, low-diversity data often learn biased, noisy semantic features, hurting both in-domain accuracy and out-of-domain robustness. Existing fixes (specialized augmentations/architectures) rely on domain assumptions that may not generalize across modalities (CT, MRI, RGB).
- Method:  Introduce Semantic Stacking (S2S2), an add-on, domain-agnostic training strategy that estimates a denoised semantic representation by stacking images that share the same ground-truth segmentation map and guiding the network toward that representation. Practically, S2S2 adds a semantic consistency loss between features from two images per mask sampled from the stack and combines it with the standard segmentation loss. The consistency loss is applied to both encoder and decoder features and instantiated as 1 − cosine similarity, improving feature robustness without changing the base architecture or augmentation pipeline. (Paper: Sec. 1, 3; Fig. 2; lines 29–31, 55–74, 89–95, 123–126; Code: TransUNet trainer.py utils.ConsistencyLoss)

### Seed 31785eaae78c [CV] — "Low-Resource Vision Challenges for Foundation Models"
- Retrieved via: Recognition and segmentation degrade when feature map statistics shift because patch token distributions, spatial resolution, or object scales at test time differ from training, causing anchor box priors and resulting segmentation masks to fail without target-domain samples.
- Problem: Foundation models tokenize images into large patches, missing the subtle, fine-grained local details that distinguish classes in specialized low-resource domains.
- Method:  Without retraining the backbone, split the pre-trained ViT patch-embedding linear projection kernel K (e.g., q=14) into smaller sub-kernels (u=7, T=49) and apply them to subregions of each original patch to capture fine-grained local patterns. Learn a lightweight weighting vector to fuse sub-kernel feature maps into a patch-level feature, then max-pool and flatten so the token dimensionality aligns with the frozen foundation model. This parameter-efficient tokenization enhances attention to small local cues and outperforms state-of-the-art fine-grained methods under severe data scarcity.

### Seed ec79896a1fe8 [ROBOTICS] — "Meta Reinforcement Learning for Sim-to-real Domain Adaptation"
- Retrieved via: A learned policy and value function fail to control reliably when deployment dynamics or reward signal distributions differ from those seen in training rollouts, and trajectories from the target MDP are unavailable during learning.
- Problem: Policies trained in simulation often fail to transfer to real robots under unknown or varying dynamics; domain-randomized policies can adapt unpredictably and even deteriorate during on-policy updates on hardware.
- Method:  Use a gradient-based meta-reinforcement learning framework (MAML-like) that trains a meta-policy across tasks defined by randomized dynamics, explicitly optimizing expected return after N inner-loop adaptation steps. At deployment, the policy performs K-rollout inner-loop updates with vanilla policy gradient to quickly adapt to the current dynamics, while the outer meta-update backpropagates through the inner steps and is optimized with PPO. Experiments in simulation and on a KUKA puck-hitting task show stable, consistent adaptation and better performance than a domain-randomization baseline.

### Seed c0f89de47a61 [RL] — "Speeding up Policy Simulation in Supply Chain RL"
- Retrieved via: A learned policy and value function fail to control reliably when deployment dynamics or reward signal distributions differ from those seen in training rollouts, and trajectories from the target MDP are unavailable during learning.
- Problem: In supply chain RL (and many long-horizon MDPs), simulating a single trajectory under a state-dependent policy is a bottleneck because policy evaluations are inherently serial and expensive (often DNN-based), while standard parallel discrete event methods like Time Warp suffer rollbacks and GPU-unfriendly message passing.
- Method:  Introduce Picard iteration for policy simulation: split the horizon [T] across M (virtual) processes and initialize a cache of actions for all time-steps. In each iteration, every process sweeps the entire trajectory but evaluates the policy π only on its assigned time-steps, using cached actions elsewhere (or aϕ if infeasible), while applying the cheap system transition f at every step; at iteration end, each process writes its evaluated actions back to the cache. This enables batched, GPU-friendly evaluation of π with no message passing and provably converges to the sequential trajectory in at most T iterations (Algorithm 1; correctness Proposition 2.1; batching and speedup discussion).

### Seed 3065495cf523 [RL] — "Conformal Constrained Policy Optimization for Cost-Effective LLM Agents"
- Retrieved via: A learned policy and value function fail to control reliably when deployment dynamics or reward signal distributions differ from those seen in training rollouts, and trajectories from the target MDP are unavailable during learning.
- Problem: Directly learning set-valued (conformal) policies is combinatorial, and the distribution shift between behavior and target policies complicates reliable coverage calibration in sequential settings.
- Method:  Introduce CCPO, which parameterizes a score policy π(a|o) and a threshold κ, and defines a stochastic surrogate S_{π,κ} that places uniform probability over the conformal action set C_{π,κ}(o). Train π with an actor–critic using V-trace off-policy corrections from π to S_{π,κ} with clipped importance weights, and perform a trust-region CPO update that optimizes advantages under a KL constraint while enforcing an upper-bounded coverage constraint; replace the non-differentiable indicator in C with a sigmoid softmask for stable gradients. Update κ online via decaying-step-size conformal prediction to track the target 1−α coverage, followed by a final batch calibration step; under mild assumptions this yields asymptotic 1−α coverage (and O(T^{-1/2+ξ}) coverage regret in adversarial streams) while minimizing cost.

### Seed cdb65a898b67 [IR_REC] — "FIRST: Faster Improved Listwise Reranking with Single Token Decoding"
- Retrieved via: Retrieval pipelines suffer recall@k drops when the serving-time query distribution or document domain drifts from the embedding index and candidate pool used in training, and target-domain queries and click logs needed to stabilize query-document scores and cross-encoder reranking are unavailable during training.
- Problem: Inference-time relevance feedback for retrievers typically relies on cross-encoder scores and KL divergence, but listwise LLM rerankers output only orderings, making standard distillation inapplicable and potentially missing stronger supervision signals.
- Method:  Use the LLM reranker’s ranked ordering as the supervision signal for updating retriever query embeddings, optimizing with the weighted RankNet loss over retrieved candidates to convert orderings into a differentiable objective. This enables relevance feedback without numeric scores and leverages the stronger listwise signal. On BEIR, LLM-based feedback improves recall@100 over cross-encoder feedback (e.g., 71.2% vs 69.0% average), and combining both signals yields further gains (72.0%).

### Seed aee1f702cc44 [IR_REC] — "Fast Offline Policy Optimization for Large Scale Recommendation"
- Retrieved via: Retrieval pipelines suffer recall@k drops when the serving-time query distribution or document domain drifts from the embedding index and candidate pool used in training, and target-domain queries and click logs needed to stabilize query-document scores and cross-encoder reranking are unavailable during training.
- Problem: Serving-time constraints require millisecond top-1 retrieval from large catalogs, and training–serving mismatch or learning full item embeddings can hurt stability and performance.
- Method:  They parameterize the policy with a dot-product form fθ(a,x)=hθ(x)ᵀβa and assume fixed item embeddings β, learning only the user transform hθ(x). A single approximate MIPS index over β is built once and reused both at inference (argmax retrieval) and during training to construct q_{ε,K}, aligning optimization with deployment. Empirically, fixing β has negligible training-time cost and improves performance versus training β (Table 2), supporting the design choice.

### Seed 51fd874583a1 [IR_REC] — "History-Aware Conversational Dense Retrieval"
- Retrieved via: Retrieval pipelines suffer recall@k drops when the serving-time query distribution or document domain drifts from the embedding index and candidate pool used in training, and target-domain queries and click logs needed to stabilize query-document scores and cross-encoder reranking are unavailable during training.
- Problem: A single ground-truth passage per query provides weak supervision for training conversational retrievers, especially when reformulated queries still contain residual noise.
- Method:  HAConvDR mines additional supervision from history using the same PRJs: historical passages labeled relevant become pseudo positives, while those labeled irrelevant serve as historical hard negatives. It then performs history-aware contrastive learning in a dual-encoder setup, combining these pseudo positives and historical hard negatives with standard in-batch/BM25 negatives. This teaches the model to leverage helpful historical passages and avoid harmful ones, alleviating shortcut dependency and strengthening retrieval training. In implementation, the passage encoder is frozen and the query encoder is updated, with one pseudo positive and one historical hard negative sampled per instance for efficiency.

### Seed 26af209a8743 [SPEECH] — "Multiresolution and Multimodal Speech Recognition with Transformers"
- Retrieved via: ASR systems incur WER spikes and unstable phoneme alignment when acoustic frame statistics shift due to unseen speakers, microphones, or noise profiles at test time, and representative spectrograms from the target condition are unavailable during training.
- Problem: ASR target choices (characters vs subwords) have complementary error patterns; single-resolution training can overfit and underperform on rare words or syntax.
- Method:  Use multiresolution multitask learning: a shared Transformer decoder predicts both character- and subword-level transcriptions, with separate output layers, trained via a weighted sum of losses (γ controls the balance; γ=0.5 performs best). This acts as regularization and reduces decoding steps for subwords while retaining fine-grained character supervision, yielding around 50% faster convergence and up to ~18% relative WER improvement over subword-only models on How2.

### Seed 1905468c9d3f [SPEECH] — "RED-ACE: Robust Error Detection for ASR using Confidence Embeddings"
- Retrieved via: ASR systems incur WER spikes and unstable phoneme alignment when acoustic frame statistics shift due to unseen speakers, microphones, or noise profiles at test time, and representative spectrograms from the target condition are unavailable during training.
- Problem: ASR error detection models typically rely only on the transcription text and ignore auxiliary ASR signals like word-level confidences, limiting their ability to accurately flag recognition errors.
- Method:  Introduce RED-ACE, a BERT-based sequence tagger augmented with a confidence embedding layer. ASR word-level confidence scores are quantized into bins and mapped via a learned embedding matrix; the confidence embedding is summed with the token, segment, and position embeddings to form each token’s input. This joint, contextualized encoding of text and confidence yields statistically significant F1 gains over text-only BERT and over naive score fusion (e.g., appending raw scores, BERT C) on LibriSpeech clean/other (e.g., +4.9% and +7.4% F1), remains robust under cross-pool shifts (+6.3% and +9.9% F1), and generalizes across different ASR models (+2.6% and +3.8% F1).

### Seed c36ac00d94ea [SPEECH] — "Unlocking Speech Instruction Data Potential with Query Rewriting"
- Retrieved via: ASR systems incur WER spikes and unstable phoneme alignment when acoustic frame statistics shift due to unseen speakers, microphones, or noise profiles at test time, and representative spectrograms from the target condition are unavailable during training.
- Problem: Validating that TTS-synthesized speech preserves the linguistic content of the source text is unreliable with a single ASR or WER-only checks; ASR errors lead to improper filtering and dataset bias.
- Method:  Use multi-agent annotation and verification: transcribe each synthesized audio with three diverse ASR models (whisper-large-v3, canary-1b, parakeet-tdt-1.1b) and compute semantic similarity to the original text using three embedding models (gte-large-en-v1.5, mxbai-embed-large-v1, stella_en_400M_v5). Average across ASRs and embeddings to form a SIM score and filter with a threshold (α=0.9). This ensemble reduces annotation errors and aligns well with WER (e.g., >98% consistency when WER=0) and improves WER over single-ASR selection (Table 4).

### Seed 5b1967bcefff [ROBOTICS] — "SimLauncher: Launching Sample-Efficient Real-world Robotic Reinforcement Learning via Simulation Pre-training"
- Retrieved via: In sim-to-real transfer, a manipulation policy within a sensorimotor loop fails when real-world dynamics and contact properties deviate from the training dynamics model, and data from the target platform needed for contact reasoning is unavailable during training.
- Problem: Transferring visuomotor policies from simulation to the real world is hindered by visual/physical sim-to-real gaps, and critics can overfit if trained only on simulation data.
- Method:  The framework builds task-specific digital twins and trains policies via a privileged-to-visual distillation pipeline: learn a state-based (privileged) policy with RL in sim, generate successful trajectories, then behavior clone a visuomotor policy using RGB + proprio. It mitigates sim-to-real gaps by calibrating physics/cameras, randomizing camera pose in sim demos, applying visual augmentations during BC, and masking backgrounds at train/test time with SAM2-tracked segmentations. A small number of real rollouts from the pre-trained policy are added to the demo buffer and sampled equally with simulated demos to regularize the critic and prevent value underestimation on real transitions.

### Seed ec79896a1fe8 [ROBOTICS] — "Meta Reinforcement Learning for Sim-to-real Domain Adaptation"
- Retrieved via: In sim-to-real transfer, a manipulation policy within a sensorimotor loop fails when real-world dynamics and contact properties deviate from the training dynamics model, and data from the target platform needed for contact reasoning is unavailable during training.
- Problem: Policies trained in simulation often fail to transfer to real robots under unknown or varying dynamics; domain-randomized policies can adapt unpredictably and even deteriorate during on-policy updates on hardware.
- Method:  Use a gradient-based meta-reinforcement learning framework (MAML-like) that trains a meta-policy across tasks defined by randomized dynamics, explicitly optimizing expected return after N inner-loop adaptation steps. At deployment, the policy performs K-rollout inner-loop updates with vanilla policy gradient to quickly adapt to the current dynamics, while the outer meta-update backpropagates through the inner steps and is optimized with PPO. Experiments in simulation and on a KUKA puck-hitting task show stable, consistent adaptation and better performance than a domain-randomization baseline.

### Seed 7e5bd0ffdeba [ROBOTICS] — "TwinTac: A Wide-Range, Highly Sensitive Tactile Sensor with Real-to-Sim Digital Twin Sensor Model"
- Retrieved via: In sim-to-real transfer, a manipulation policy within a sensorimotor loop fails when real-world dynamics and contact properties deviate from the training dynamics model, and data from the target platform needed for contact reasoning is unavailable during training.
- Problem: There is a lack of high-fidelity simulators for non-optical tactile sensors, hindering simulation-driven policy learning and data generation.
- Method:  DigiTac is a digital twin built via a Real2Sim pipeline: an FEM-based elastomer model is created in Isaac Gym (soft-body mesh with 7636 vertices), and synchronized real/sim data are collected (FEM node positions/stresses X, simulated force F_sim, real MEMS outputs S, real force F_real). Dynamic Time Warping aligns real and simulated sequences. To map FEM outputs to sensor readings, the method aggregates Von Mises stress features from a 609-node subset located above the 8 taxels and feeds them to an MLP trained with L1 loss (AdamW, noise injection). The model reproduces PhysTac with an average error of 5.95% of the maximum contact pressure. (Supported: lines 80–87, 94–107, 110–123, 116–121, 152).

You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
  Score 2: token pruning with a different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of known pruning method.

### Block B — "compositional reasoning with discrete latent structure"
  Score 5: discrete latent composition graphs induced via learned
           merge policy + RL reward shaped by downstream task.
  Score 4: VQ-VAE codes as discrete symbols + neural composition.
  Score 3: ST Gumbel-Softmax for discrete tree induction.
  Score 2: VQ-VAE with renamed quantization function.
  Score 1: standard transformer over text.

### Block C — "time-series anomaly detection in irregular sequences"
  Score 5: time-aware transformer with continuous-time positional
           encoding + downstream-cost-tuned anomaly threshold via
           RL on alarm precision/recall trade-off.
  Score 4: Neural CDE encoder + density-ratio anomaly score —
           combination not surfaced as joint application.
  Score 3: ODE-RNN encoder with reconstruction-error scoring —
           established pattern.
  Score 2: GRU autoencoder on resampled regular intervals with a
           different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of an LSTM forecasting + z-score
           baseline.

## ReAct loop — output format each round (no markdown)

Each round, output EXACTLY ONE of these two formats:

Search format (issue when more evidence is needed):
    Thought: <1-3 sentences on which mechanism pairing you want to
              verify in prior work>
    Query: <semantic scholar search keywords>

Finalize format (issue ONLY when ready to score):
    Reasoning: <2-4 sentences synthesizing what searches found and
                how it maps to a score anchor>
    Prior works: <newline-separated list of papers/methods that most
                  directly cover the proposed method, one per line
                  in the form "paper_title or method_name (year): one-line
                  summary of overlap with the proposed method".
                  Use "(none)" if score is 5 with no near-clone surfaced.>
    Score: <integer 1-5>

Hard rules:
  - DO NOT mix the search and finalize formats. If you output a
    `Query:` line, the caller treats this round as a search and
    ignores any `Score:` you write — do not waste tokens on Score
    in a search round.
  - You MUST issue at least one search before finalizing UNLESS the
    method is a trivial restatement of a known technique.
  - When you write the literal token "Score:" inside Reasoning to
    cite an anchor, write it INLINE (e.g., "matches the Score 4
    anchor"), never on its own line — the parser anchors to lines
    starting with "Score:".
  - Prior works lines must each start with "  - " (two spaces, dash,
    space) so the parser can extract them. Do NOT include a trailing
    "Score:" inside a Prior works line.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Test-time distributions diverge from training data along axes
the model never saw; how can we make models robust to such
shifts without observing the target distribution at training?


### Proposed method
We propose Robust-Without-Target (RWT), a two-stage recipe that combines training-time shift-agnostic regularization with a test-time adapter and safety wrapper. At training, we (a) replace deterministic data dropout/reweighting with a probabilistic, influence-bounded sampler that caps the gradient of per-example sampling probabilities; and (b) add a multiresolution auxiliary head to regularize features across granularities. Concretely, we compute per-example influence signals on a held-out split and sample with a linear/sigmoid function whose slope σ bounds sensitivity; this mimics distributionally robust training within a χ²-ball around the empirical distribution while avoiding overconfidence on the validation mix. In parallel, we train a light auxiliary prediction head at a coarser or alternative resolution (e.g., class clusters, attribute tags, or subword/character analogs), applying a weighted multi-task loss to stabilize semantics across unseen axes.

Next, we meta-learn a test-time adaptation operator across synthetic shifts, without access to the true target distribution. We define a set of plausible shift generators (e.g., style/texture perturbations, resolution changes, occlusion/masking, label-imbalance resampling) and construct inner–outer loops: an inner loop performs a few test-time steps using unsupervised/self-supervised objectives (entropy minimization, consistency across augmentations, and agreement between main and auxiliary heads); the outer loop optimizes the base model so that post-adaptation performance on held-out views improves. This yields a MAML-like adapter that can quickly specialize to novel conditions using only the incoming test stream.

At inference, we attach a training-free, non-parametric error-compensation memory. The system stores a rolling buffer of recent test representations, base predictions, and any available outcomes; when labels are unavailable or delayed, it stores proxy nonconformity scores (e.g., softmax margin, disagreement across augmentations/heads). For each new input, we retrieve similar states by attention over the buffer and estimate a residual bias to add to the base prediction, with a temperature and clamp to keep corrections bounded. The buffer is refreshed online, enabling self-correction even under rapid drift.

Finally, we wrap predictions with an online-calibrated conformal safety layer. We score each output with a monotone, model-derived nonconformity (e.g., 1 − max softmax or margin) and maintain a threshold κ updated with a decaying-step-size rule to target 1−α coverage. Below κ we return a point prediction; above κ we either return a small set (top-k covering set) or abstain, depending on the application. This provides coverage guarantees and graceful degradation under unforeseen shifts. The full system requires no target-domain samples at training: robustness comes from influence-bounded sampling and multiresolution regularization, quick specialization is delivered by the meta-learned adapter, rapid local bias correction comes from the error memory, and reliability is enforced by online conformal calibration.

### Rationale
Unknown, out-of-support shifts can break models along axes unseen in training. RWT addresses this in layered fashion: (1) bounded-gradient probabilistic sampling reduces the model’s sensitivity to any one training mix and improves worst-case risk within a principled uncertainty set, while multiresolution multitask losses pull features toward semantics that persist across scales, reducing overfit to spurious axes; (2) a meta-learned adapter trains the model to improve after a small, unsupervised adaptation step on synthetic, diverse perturbations, enabling quick specialization at deployment without target-domain pre-exposure; (3) a non-parametric error memory adds fast, training-free local corrections that follow the current stream, even with weak or delayed supervision; and (4) conformal calibration converts raw scores into coverage-controlled decisions, ensuring reliability despite residual mismatch.

Because no single mechanism fully protects against unseen axes, the combination is key: influence-bounded training and multiresolution regularization make the base model broadly stable; the adapter and memory handle rapid or structural drifts at test time; and conformal thresholds bound risk when uncertainty spikes. Crucially, none of these components require observing the true target distribution during training.

### Inspired seeds (the ideation's input retrieval)
1. [IR_REC] Less Is Better: Unweighted Data Subsampling via Influence Function (1912.01321)
   problem: Influence-based deterministic ‘dropout’ sampling can become over-confident on a specific validation distribution, hurting out-of-sample generalization under distribution shift.
   method:  Develop probabilistic sampling functions with bounded gradients to control confidence and improve robustness across distributions within a χ²-divergence ball around the empirical distribution. Theoretically, they prove the worst-case risk over this uncertainty set is Lipschitz in the influence vector with constant scaling as O(σ√((2δ+1)/n)), where σ bounds the sampling function’s gradient (Theorem 3), highlighting instability of deterministic dropout (unbounded gradient). They also introduce a surrogate confidence metric Γ(φ)=‖θ_ε−θ‖² and show it shares the same σ-dependent change rate (Theorem 4), and instantiate practical Linear and Sigmoid sampling functions that tune σ via a single parameter.

2. [LLM_NLP] On Training Data Influence of GPT Models (2404.07840)
   problem: Training data attribution (TDA) for autoregressive GPT models has focused mostly on test loss and often ties simulator parameters to seen training indices, limiting generalization to unseen data and overlooking richer training dynamics.
   method:  GPTfluence introduces a featurized simulator that models training as an n-th order Markov process with multiplicative and additive influence factors. It computes per-batch effects via α and β, which are aggregated from per-example factors A_i,j and B_i derived from pairwise interactions between frozen pre-trained text encodings of training and test examples. Using a pre-trained encoder (e.g., BERT/GPT) for both training and test inputs enables parameter sharing over features rather than indices, allowing autoregressive prediction of full performance trajectories and robust generalization to unseen training/test data.

3. [IR_REC] ReLoop2: Building Self-Adaptive Recommendation Models via Responsive Error Compensation Loop (2306.08808)
   problem: Online recommender systems face non-stationary data and test-time distribution shift, while gradient-based incremental updates adapt too slowly for large CTR models.
   method:  ReLoop2 introduces a slow–fast learning paradigm with a training-free, non-parametric error memory that stores recent error samples (hidden state, base prediction, and ground-truth) observed during serving. At inference, it retrieves similar samples to the current hidden representation and uses attention with a temperature to estimate the prediction error; this error is then used to compensate the base model’s output with a tunable weight and clamping to [0,1]. The approach continually refreshes the memory online, creating a self-correcting loop for fast test-time adaptation, and is model-agnostic and orthogonal to incremental training.

4. [MULTIMODAL] Plug-and-Play VQA: Zero-shot VQA by Conjoining Large Pretrained Models with Zero Training (2210.08773)
   problem: Zero-shot VQA needs to reason over images and questions without any additional multimodal training; generic, image-wide captions often miss question-relevant details, limiting a text-only QA model’s effectiveness.
   method:  Use a pretrained VLM (BLIP) both to localize question-relevant regions and to generate diverse, question-guided captions. Relevance is computed by a GradCAM-style aggregation over BLIP’s cross-attention in the Image-grounded Text Encoder: multiply cross-attention scores by gradients of the image–text similarity and average over heads/tokens to get per-patch relevance, then sample K′ patches per caption proportionally to this relevance. From these patches, generate N diverse captions via stochastic top-k decoding (e.g., k=50) and drop near-duplicates. This produces captions with higher answer-hit rates and boosts zero-shot VQA accuracy, outperforming generic/random patch captioning and even 5 human captions when using 100 question-guided captions.

5. [MULTIMODAL] Visual Semantic Reasoning for Image-Text Matching (1909.02701)
   problem: Pure metric learning may not sufficiently enforce that the learned image embeddings encode captionable semantics needed for robust cross-modal alignment.
   method:  Jointly optimize a hinge-based triplet ranking loss with hard negatives in the shared embedding space (using simple inner-product similarity) together with an auxiliary sequence-to-sequence caption generation loss with attention on the same visual features. This multi-objective training encourages embeddings that are both discriminative for retrieval and predictive of captions, improving R@1 over training without the generation objective.

6. [MULTIMODAL] DetCLIPv2: Scalable Open-Vocabulary Object Detection Pre-training via Word-Region Alignment (2304.04514)
   problem: Open-vocabulary detection needs to learn from massive web image–text pairs, but captions lack instance-level boxes, VL distillation suffers domain gaps, and pseudo-labeling limits accuracy and scalability.
   method:  DetCLIPv2 performs end-to-end joint pre-training on detection, grounding, and raw image–text pairs under a unified formulation, learning fine-grained word–region alignment directly from captions. For each noun phrase, it matches the best region proposal via a max-similarity (1-to-1) set alignment and optimizes a text-to-image contrastive loss only, which mitigates the partial-label problem in web data. It selects proposals for contrast by scoring regions with the maximum similarity to all concepts in the local batch, focusing on text-relevant regions. Alternating data types during training creates a flywheel between localization and recognition, and using low-resolution inputs for web pairs enables efficient scaling (e.g., 13× more pairs with similar training time).

7. [CV] Robust Instance Segmentation through Reasoning about Multi-Object Occlusion (2012.02107)
   problem: Per-object, per-pixel feed-forward segmentations often conflict in overlap regions (multiple objects claiming the same pixels), especially under heavy or same-category occlusions, leading to erroneous masks and classifications.
   method:  Introduce an Occlusion Reasoning Module (ORM) that operates on the per-object likelihood maps. It detects conflicts where two objects both predict foreground, resolves them via pixel-level competition by assigning each contested pixel to the object with higher foreground likelihood, and then recovers the pairwise occlusion order by comparing the number of reassigned pixels in the conflict set. Using this order, it enforces consistent labeling in overlaps and feeds the corrected occlusion priors back into the CompositionalNet to mask occluded features and recompute class likelihoods, iterating this top-down self-correction to improve both segmentation and classification under occlusion.

8. [CV] S2S2: Semantic Stacking for Robust Semantic Segmentation in Medical Imaging (2412.13156)
   problem: Medical image segmentation models trained on scarce, low-diversity data often learn biased, noisy semantic features, hurting both in-domain accuracy and out-of-domain robustness. Existing fixes (specialized augmentations/architectures) rely on domain assumptions that may not generalize across modalities (CT, MRI, RGB).
   method:  Introduce Semantic Stacking (S2S2), an add-on, domain-agnostic training strategy that estimates a denoised semantic representation by stacking images that share the same ground-truth segmentation map and guiding the network toward that representation. Practically, S2S2 adds a semantic consistency loss between features from two images per mask sampled from the stack and combines it with the standard segmentation loss. The consistency loss is applied to both encoder and decoder features and instantiated as 1 − cosine similarity, improving feature robustness without changing the base architecture or augmentation pipeline. (Paper: Sec. 1, 3; Fig. 2; lines 29–31, 55–74, 89–95, 123–126; Code: TransUNet trainer.py utils.ConsistencyLoss)

9. [CV] Low-Resource Vision Challenges for Foundation Models (2401.04716)
   problem: Foundation models tokenize images into large patches, missing the subtle, fine-grained local details that distinguish classes in specialized low-resource domains.
   method:  Without retraining the backbone, split the pre-trained ViT patch-embedding linear projection kernel K (e.g., q=14) into smaller sub-kernels (u=7, T=49) and apply them to subregions of each original patch to capture fine-grained local patterns. Learn a lightweight weighting vector to fuse sub-kernel feature maps into a patch-level feature, then max-pool and flatten so the token dimensionality aligns with the frozen foundation model. This parameter-efficient tokenization enhances attention to small local cues and outperforms state-of-the-art fine-grained methods under severe data scarcity.

10. [ROBOTICS] Meta Reinforcement Learning for Sim-to-real Domain Adaptation (1909.12906)
   problem: Policies trained in simulation often fail to transfer to real robots under unknown or varying dynamics; domain-randomized policies can adapt unpredictably and even deteriorate during on-policy updates on hardware.
   method:  Use a gradient-based meta-reinforcement learning framework (MAML-like) that trains a meta-policy across tasks defined by randomized dynamics, explicitly optimizing expected return after N inner-loop adaptation steps. At deployment, the policy performs K-rollout inner-loop updates with vanilla policy gradient to quickly adapt to the current dynamics, while the outer meta-update backpropagates through the inner steps and is optimized with PPO. Experiments in simulation and on a KUKA puck-hitting task show stable, consistent adaptation and better performance than a domain-randomization baseline.

11. [RL] Speeding up Policy Simulation in Supply Chain RL (2406.01939)
   problem: In supply chain RL (and many long-horizon MDPs), simulating a single trajectory under a state-dependent policy is a bottleneck because policy evaluations are inherently serial and expensive (often DNN-based), while standard parallel discrete event methods like Time Warp suffer rollbacks and GPU-unfriendly message passing.
   method:  Introduce Picard iteration for policy simulation: split the horizon [T] across M (virtual) processes and initialize a cache of actions for all time-steps. In each iteration, every process sweeps the entire trajectory but evaluates the policy π only on its assigned time-steps, using cached actions elsewhere (or aϕ if infeasible), while applying the cheap system transition f at every step; at iteration end, each process writes its evaluated actions back to the cache. This enables batched, GPU-friendly evaluation of π with no message passing and provably converges to the sequential trajectory in at most T iterations (Algorithm 1; correctness Proposition 2.1; batching and speedup discussion).

12. [RL] Conformal Constrained Policy Optimization for Cost-Effective LLM Agents (2511.11828)
   problem: Directly learning set-valued (conformal) policies is combinatorial, and the distribution shift between behavior and target policies complicates reliable coverage calibration in sequential settings.
   method:  Introduce CCPO, which parameterizes a score policy π(a|o) and a threshold κ, and defines a stochastic surrogate S_{π,κ} that places uniform probability over the conformal action set C_{π,κ}(o). Train π with an actor–critic using V-trace off-policy corrections from π to S_{π,κ} with clipped importance weights, and perform a trust-region CPO update that optimizes advantages under a KL constraint while enforcing an upper-bounded coverage constraint; replace the non-differentiable indicator in C with a sigmoid softmask for stable gradients. Update κ online via decaying-step-size conformal prediction to track the target 1−α coverage, followed by a final batch calibration step; under mild assumptions this yields asymptotic 1−α coverage (and O(T^{-1/2+ξ}) coverage regret in adversarial streams) while minimizing cost.

13. [IR_REC] FIRST: Faster Improved Listwise Reranking with Single Token Decoding (2406.15657)
   problem: Inference-time relevance feedback for retrievers typically relies on cross-encoder scores and KL divergence, but listwise LLM rerankers output only orderings, making standard distillation inapplicable and potentially missing stronger supervision signals.
   method:  Use the LLM reranker’s ranked ordering as the supervision signal for updating retriever query embeddings, optimizing with the weighted RankNet loss over retrieved candidates to convert orderings into a differentiable objective. This enables relevance feedback without numeric scores and leverages the stronger listwise signal. On BEIR, LLM-based feedback improves recall@100 over cross-encoder feedback (e.g., 71.2% vs 69.0% average), and combining both signals yields further gains (72.0%).

14. [IR_REC] Fast Offline Policy Optimization for Large Scale Recommendation (2208.05327)
   problem: Serving-time constraints require millisecond top-1 retrieval from large catalogs, and training–serving mismatch or learning full item embeddings can hurt stability and performance.
   method:  They parameterize the policy with a dot-product form fθ(a,x)=hθ(x)ᵀβa and assume fixed item embeddings β, learning only the user transform hθ(x). A single approximate MIPS index over β is built once and reused both at inference (argmax retrieval) and during training to construct q_{ε,K}, aligning optimization with deployment. Empirically, fixing β has negligible training-time cost and improves performance versus training β (Table 2), supporting the design choice.

15. [IR_REC] History-Aware Conversational Dense Retrieval (2401.16659)
   problem: A single ground-truth passage per query provides weak supervision for training conversational retrievers, especially when reformulated queries still contain residual noise.
   method:  HAConvDR mines additional supervision from history using the same PRJs: historical passages labeled relevant become pseudo positives, while those labeled irrelevant serve as historical hard negatives. It then performs history-aware contrastive learning in a dual-encoder setup, combining these pseudo positives and historical hard negatives with standard in-batch/BM25 negatives. This teaches the model to leverage helpful historical passages and avoid harmful ones, alleviating shortcut dependency and strengthening retrieval training. In implementation, the passage encoder is frozen and the query encoder is updated, with one pseudo positive and one historical hard negative sampled per instance for efficiency.

16. [SPEECH] Multiresolution and Multimodal Speech Recognition with Transformers (2004.14840)
   problem: ASR target choices (characters vs subwords) have complementary error patterns; single-resolution training can overfit and underperform on rare words or syntax.
   method:  Use multiresolution multitask learning: a shared Transformer decoder predicts both character- and subword-level transcriptions, with separate output layers, trained via a weighted sum of losses (γ controls the balance; γ=0.5 performs best). This acts as regularization and reduces decoding steps for subwords while retaining fine-grained character supervision, yielding around 50% faster convergence and up to ~18% relative WER improvement over subword-only models on How2.

17. [SPEECH] RED-ACE: Robust Error Detection for ASR using Confidence Embeddings (2203.07172)
   problem: ASR error detection models typically rely only on the transcription text and ignore auxiliary ASR signals like word-level confidences, limiting their ability to accurately flag recognition errors.
   method:  Introduce RED-ACE, a BERT-based sequence tagger augmented with a confidence embedding layer. ASR word-level confidence scores are quantized into bins and mapped via a learned embedding matrix; the confidence embedding is summed with the token, segment, and position embeddings to form each token’s input. This joint, contextualized encoding of text and confidence yields statistically significant F1 gains over text-only BERT and over naive score fusion (e.g., appending raw scores, BERT C) on LibriSpeech clean/other (e.g., +4.9% and +7.4% F1), remains robust under cross-pool shifts (+6.3% and +9.9% F1), and generalizes across different ASR models (+2.6% and +3.8% F1).

18. [SPEECH] Unlocking Speech Instruction Data Potential with Query Rewriting (2507.08603)
   problem: Validating that TTS-synthesized speech preserves the linguistic content of the source text is unreliable with a single ASR or WER-only checks; ASR errors lead to improper filtering and dataset bias.
   method:  Use multi-agent annotation and verification: transcribe each synthesized audio with three diverse ASR models (whisper-large-v3, canary-1b, parakeet-tdt-1.1b) and compute semantic similarity to the original text using three embedding models (gte-large-en-v1.5, mxbai-embed-large-v1, stella_en_400M_v5). Average across ASRs and embeddings to form a SIM score and filter with a threshold (α=0.9). This ensemble reduces annotation errors and aligns well with WER (e.g., >98% consistency when WER=0) and improves WER over single-ASR selection (Table 4).

19. [ROBOTICS] SimLauncher: Launching Sample-Efficient Real-world Robotic Reinforcement Learning via Simulation Pre-training (2507.04452)
   problem: Transferring visuomotor policies from simulation to the real world is hindered by visual/physical sim-to-real gaps, and critics can overfit if trained only on simulation data.
   method:  The framework builds task-specific digital twins and trains policies via a privileged-to-visual distillation pipeline: learn a state-based (privileged) policy with RL in sim, generate successful trajectories, then behavior clone a visuomotor policy using RGB + proprio. It mitigates sim-to-real gaps by calibrating physics/cameras, randomizing camera pose in sim demos, applying visual augmentations during BC, and masking backgrounds at train/test time with SAM2-tracked segmentations. A small number of real rollouts from the pre-trained policy are added to the demo buffer and sampled equally with simulated demos to regularize the critic and prevent value underestimation on real transitions.

20. [ROBOTICS] Meta Reinforcement Learning for Sim-to-real Domain Adaptation (1909.12906)
   problem: Policies trained in simulation often fail to transfer to real robots under unknown or varying dynamics; domain-randomized policies can adapt unpredictably and even deteriorate during on-policy updates on hardware.
   method:  Use a gradient-based meta-reinforcement learning framework (MAML-like) that trains a meta-policy across tasks defined by randomized dynamics, explicitly optimizing expected return after N inner-loop adaptation steps. At deployment, the policy performs K-rollout inner-loop updates with vanilla policy gradient to quickly adapt to the current dynamics, while the outer meta-update backpropagates through the inner steps and is optimized with PPO. Experiments in simulation and on a KUKA puck-hitting task show stable, consistent adaptation and better performance than a domain-randomization baseline.

21. [ROBOTICS] TwinTac: A Wide-Range, Highly Sensitive Tactile Sensor with Real-to-Sim Digital Twin Sensor Model (2509.10063)
   problem: There is a lack of high-fidelity simulators for non-optical tactile sensors, hindering simulation-driven policy learning and data generation.
   method:  DigiTac is a digital twin built via a Real2Sim pipeline: an FEM-based elastomer model is created in Isaac Gym (soft-body mesh with 7636 vertices), and synchronized real/sim data are collected (FEM node positions/stresses X, simulated force F_sim, real MEMS outputs S, real force F_real). Dynamic Time Warping aligns real and simulated sequences. To map FEM outputs to sensor readings, the method aggregates Von Mises stress features from a 609-node subset located above the 8 taxels and feeds them to an MLP trained with L1 loss (AdamW, noise injection). The model reproduces PhysTac with an average error of 5.95% of the maximum contact pressure. (Supported: lines 80–87, 94–107, 110–123, 116–121, 152).

## Tie-breaking
When between two adjacent scores, default to the LOWER. Resist
optimism bias toward "novel". Score against the literature
signal only; do not apply an additional penalty when the method
reuses inspired seeds (the seeds are context, not a benchmark).

Round 1/10.

Last search results:
(empty on first round — issue your first search)

Either issue a Search (Thought + Query) or Finalize (Reasoning + Prior works + Score).

messages.content:
You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
  Score 2: token pruning with a different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of known pruning method.

### Block B — "compositional reasoning with discrete latent structure"
  Score 5: discrete latent composition graphs induced via learned
           merge policy + RL reward shaped by downstream task.
  Score 4: VQ-VAE codes as discrete symbols + neural composition.
  Score 3: ST Gumbel-Softmax for discrete tree induction.
  Score 2: VQ-VAE with renamed quantization function.
  Score 1: standard transformer over text.

### Block C — "time-series anomaly detection in irregular sequences"
  Score 5: time-aware transformer with continuous-time positional
           encoding + downstream-cost-tuned anomaly threshold via
           RL on alarm precision/recall trade-off.
  Score 4: Neural CDE encoder + density-ratio anomaly score —
           combination not surfaced as joint application.
  Score 3: ODE-RNN encoder with reconstruction-error scoring —
           established pattern.
  Score 2: GRU autoencoder on resampled regular intervals with a
           different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of an LSTM forecasting + z-score
           baseline.

## ReAct loop — output format each round (no markdown)

Each round, output EXACTLY ONE of these two formats:

Search format (issue when more evidence is needed):
    Thought: <1-3 sentences on which mechanism pairing you want to
              verify in prior work>
    Query: <semantic scholar search keywords>

Finalize format (issue ONLY when ready to score):
    Reasoning: <2-4 sentences synthesizing what searches found and
                how it maps to a score anchor>
    Prior works: <newline-separated list of papers/methods that most
                  directly cover the proposed method, one per line
                  in the form "paper_title or method_name (year): one-line
                  summary of overlap with the proposed method".
                  Use "(none)" if score is 5 with no near-clone surfaced.>
    Score: <integer 1-5>

Hard rules:
  - DO NOT mix the search and finalize formats. If you output a

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Test-time distributions diverge from training data along axes
the model never saw; how can we make models robust to such
shifts without observing the target distribution at training?


### Proposed method
We propose Robust-Without-Target (RWT), a two-stage recipe that combines training-time shift-agnostic regularization with a test-time adapter and safety wrapper. At training, we (a) replace deterministic data dropout/reweighting with a probabilistic, influence-bounded sampler that caps the gradient of per-example sampling probabilities; and (b) add a multiresolution auxiliary head to regularize features across granularities. Concretely, we compute per-example influence signals on a held-out split and sample with a linear/sigmoid function whose slope σ bounds sensitivity; this mimics distributionally robust training within a χ²-ball around the empirical distribution while avoiding overconfidence on the validation mix. In parallel, we train a light auxiliary prediction head at a coarser or alternative resolution (e.g., class clusters, attribute tags, or subword/character analogs), applying a weighted multi-task loss to stabilize semantics across unseen axes.

Next, we meta-learn a test-time adaptation operator across synthetic shifts, without access to the true target distribution. We define a set of plausible shift generators (e.g., style/texture perturbations, resolution changes, occlusion/masking, label-imbalance resampling) and construct inner–outer loops: an inner loop performs a few test-time steps using unsupervised/self-supervised objectives (entropy minimization, consistency across augmentations, and agreement between main and auxiliary heads); the outer loop optimizes the base model so that post-adaptation performance on held-out views improves. This yields a MAML-like adapter that can quickly specialize to novel conditions using only the incoming test stream.

At inference, we attach a training-free, non-parametric error-compensation memory. The system stores a rolling buffer of recent test representations, base predictions, and any available outcomes; when labels are unavailable or delayed, it stores proxy nonconformity scores (e.g., softmax margin, disagreement across augmentations/heads). For each new input, we retrieve similar states by attention over the buffer and estimate a residual bias to add to the base prediction, with a temperature and clamp to keep corrections bounded. The buffer is refreshed online, enabling self-correction even under rapid drift.

Finally, we wrap predictions with an online-calibrated conformal safety layer. We score each output with a monotone, model-derived nonconformity (e.g., 1 − max softmax or margin) and maintain a threshold κ updated with a decaying-step-size rule to target 1−α coverage. Below κ we return a point prediction; above κ we either return a small set (top-k covering set) or abstain, depending on the application. This provides coverage guarantees and graceful degradation under unforeseen shifts. The full system requires no target-domain samples at training: robustness comes from influence-bounded sampling and multiresolution regularization, quick specialization is delivered by the meta-learned adapter, rapid local bias correction comes from the error memory, and reliability is enforced by online conformal calibration.

### Rationale
Unknown, out-of-support shifts can break models along axes unseen in training. RWT addresses this in layered fashion: (1) bounded-gradient probabilistic sampling reduces the model’s sensitivity to any one training mix and improves worst-case risk within a principled uncertainty set, while multiresolution multitask losses pull features toward semantics that persist across scales, reducing overfit to spurious axes; (2) a meta-learned adapter trains the model to improve after a small, unsupervised adaptation step on synthetic, diverse perturbations, enabling quick specialization at deployment without target-domain pre-exposure; (3) a non-parametric error memory adds fast, training-free local corrections that follow the current stream, even with weak or delayed supervision; and (4) conformal calibration converts raw scores into coverage-controlled decisions, ensuring reliability despite residual mismatch.

Because no single mechanism fully protects against unseen axes, the combination is key: influence-bounded training and multiresolution regularization make the base model broadly stable; the adapter and memory handle rapid or structural drifts at test time; and conformal thresholds bound risk when uncertainty spikes. Crucially, none of these components require observing the true target distribution during training.

1. [IR_REC] Less Is Better: Unweighted Data Subsampling via Influence Function (1912.01321)
   problem: Influence-based deterministic ‘dropout’ sampling can become over-confident on a specific validation distribution, hurting out-of-sample generalization under distribution shift.
   method:  Develop probabilistic sampling functions with bounded gradients to control confidence and improve robustness across distributions within a χ²-divergence ball around the empirical distribution. Theoretically, they prove the worst-case risk over this uncertainty set is Lipschitz in the influence vector with constant scaling as O(σ√((2δ+1)/n)), where σ bounds the sampling function’s gradient (Theorem 3), highlighting instability of deterministic dropout (unbounded gradient). They also introduce a surrogate confidence metric Γ(φ)=‖θ_ε−θ‖² and show it shares the same σ-dependent change rate (Theorem 4), and instantiate practical Linear and Sigmoid sampling functions that tune σ via a single parameter.

2. [LLM_NLP] On Training Data Influence of GPT Models (2404.07840)
   problem: Training data attribution (TDA) for autoregressive GPT models has focused mostly on test loss and often ties simulator parameters to seen training indices, limiting generalization to unseen data and overlooking richer training dynamics.
   method:  GPTfluence introduces a featurized simulator that models training as an n-th order Markov process with multiplicative and additive influence factors. It computes per-batch effects via α and β, which are aggregated from per-example factors A_i,j and B_i derived from pairwise interactions between frozen pre-trained text encodings of training and test examples. Using a pre-trained encoder (e.g., BERT/GPT) for both training and test inputs enables parameter sharing over features rather than indices, allowing autoregressive prediction of full performance trajectories and robust generalization to unseen training/test data.

   method:  ReLoop2 introduces a slow–fast learning paradigm with a training-free, non-parametric error memory that stores recent error samples (hidden state, base prediction, and ground-truth) observed during serving. At inference, it retrieves similar samples to the current hidden representation and uses attention with a temperature to estimate the prediction error; this error is then used to compensate the base model’s output with a tunable weight and clamping to [0,1]. Relevance is computed by a GradCAM-style aggregation over BLIP’s cross-attention in the Image-grounded Text Encoder: multiply cross-attention scores by gradients of the image–text similarity and average over heads/tokens to get per-patch relevance, then sample K′ patches per caption proportionally to this relevance. From these patches, generate N diverse captions via stochastic top-k decoding (e.g., k=50) and drop near-duplicates. Using this order, it enforces consistent labeling in overlaps and feeds the corrected occlusion priors back into the CompositionalNet to mask occluded features and recompute class likelihoods, iterating this top-down self-correction to improve both segmentation and classification under occlusion.

11. [RL] Speeding up Policy Simulation in Supply Chain RL (2406.01939)
   problem: In supply chain RL (and many long-horizon MDPs), simulating a single trajectory under a state-dependent policy is a bottleneck because policy evaluations are inherently serial and expensive (often DNN-based), while standard parallel discrete event methods like Time Warp suffer rollbacks and GPU-unfriendly message passing.
   method:  Introduce Picard iteration for policy simulation: split the horizon [T] across M (virtual) processes and initialize a cache of actions for all time-steps. In each iteration, every process sweeps the entire trajectory but evaluates the policy π only on its assigned time-steps, using cached actions elsewhere (or aϕ if infeasible), while applying the cheap system transition f at every step; at iteration end, each process writes its evaluated actions back to the cache. This enables batched, GPU-friendly evaluation of π with no message passing and provably converges to the sequential trajectory in at most T iterations (Algorithm 1; correctness Proposition 2.1; batching and speedup discussion).
