1. Test-Time Adaptation with Perturbation Consistency Learning (arXiv.org 2023, cites: 1)
   Currently, pre-trained language models (PLMs) do not cope well with the distribution shift problem, resulting in models trained on the training set failing in real test scenarios. To address this problem, the test-time adaptation (TTA) shows great potential, which updates model parameters to suit the test data at the testing time. Existing TTA methods rely on well-designed auxiliary tasks or self-training strategies based on pseudo-label. However, these methods do not achieve good trade-offs regarding performance gains and computational costs. To obtain some insights into such a dilemma, we take two representative TTA methods, i.e., Tent and OIL, for exploration and find that stable prediction is the key to achieving a good balance. Accordingly, in this paper, we propose perturbation consistency learning (PCL), a simple test-time adaptation method to promote the model to make stable predictions for samples with distribution shifts. Extensive experiments on adversarial robustness and cross-lingual transferring demonstrate that our method can achieve higher or comparable performance with less inference time over strong PLM backbones and previous state-of-the-art TTA methods.

2. FRET: Feature Redundancy Elimination for Test Time Adaptation (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 2)
   Test-Time Adaptation (TTA) aims to enhance the generalization of deep learning models when faced with test data that exhibits distribution shifts from the training data. In this context, only a pre-trained model and unlabeled test data are available, making it particularly relevant for privacy-sensitive applications. In practice, we observe that feature redundancy in embeddings tends to increase as domain shifts intensify in TTA. However, existing TTA methods often overlook this redundancy, which can hinder the model's adaptability to new data. To address this issue, we introduce Feature Redundancy Elimination for Test-time Adaptation (FRET), a novel perspective for TTA. A straightforward approach (S-FRET) is to directly minimize the feature redundancy score as an optimization objective to improve adaptation. Despite its simplicity and effectiveness, S-FRET struggles with label shifts, limiting its robustness in real-world scenarios. To mitigate this limitation, we further propose Graph-based FRET (G-FRET), which integrates a Graph Convolutional Network (GCN) with contrastive learning. This design not only reduces feature redundancy but also enhances feature discriminability in both the representation and prediction layers. Extensive experiments across multiple model architectures, tasks, and datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of S-FRET and show that G-FRET achieves state-of-the-art performance. Further analysis reveals that G-FRET enables the model to extract non-redundant and highly discriminative features during inference, thereby facilitating more robust test-time adaptation.

3. pFedBBN: A Personalized Federated Test-Time Adaptation with Balanced Batch Normalization for Class-Imbalanced Data (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 0)
   Test-time adaptation (TTA) in federated learning (FL) is crucial for handling unseen data distributions across clients, particularly when faced with domain shifts and skewed class distributions. Class Imbalance (CI) remains a fundamental challenge in FL, where rare but critical classes are often severely underrepresented in individual client datasets. Although prior work has addressed CI during training through reliable aggregation and local class distribution alignment, these methods typically rely on access to labeled data or coordination among clients, and none address class unsupervised adaptation to dynamic domains or distribution shifts at inference time under federated CI constraints. Revealing the failure of state-of-the-art TTA in federated client adaptation in CI scenario, we propose pFedBBN,a personalized federated test-time adaptation framework that employs balanced batch normalization (BBN) during local client adaptation to mitigate prediction bias by treating all classes equally, while also enabling client collaboration guided by BBN similarity, ensuring that clients with similar balanced representations reinforce each other and that adaptation remains aligned with domain-specific characteristics. pFedBBN supports fully unsupervised local adaptation and introduces a class-aware model aggregation strategy that enables personalized inference without compromising privacy. It addresses both distribution shifts and class imbalance through balanced feature normalization and domain-aware collaboration, without requiring any labeled or raw data from clients. Extensive experiments across diverse baselines show that pFedBBN consistently enhances robustness and minority-class performance over state-of-the-art FL and TTA methods.

5. Order-Aware Test-Time Adaptation: Leveraging Temporal Dynamics for Robust Streaming Inference (arXiv.org 2026, cites: 0)
   Test-Time Adaptation (TTA) enables pre-trained models to adjust to distribution shift by learning from unlabeled test-time streams. However, existing methods typically treat these streams as independent samples, overlooking the supervisory signal inherent in temporal dynamics. To address this, we introduce Order-Aware Test-Time Adaptation (OATTA). We formulate test-time adaptation as a gradient-free recursive Bayesian estimation task, using a learned dynamic transition matrix as a temporal prior to refine the base model's predictions. To ensure safety in weakly structured streams, we introduce a likelihood-ratio gate (LLR) that reverts to the base predictor when temporal evidence is absent. OATTA is a lightweight, model-agnostic module that incurs negligible computational overhead. Extensive experiments across image classification, wearable and physiological signal analysis, and language sentiment analysis demonstrate its universality; OATTA consistently boosts established baselines, improving accuracy by up to 6.35%. Our findings establish that modeling temporal dynamics provides a critical, orthogonal signal beyond standard order-agnostic TTA approaches.

   Electroencephalogram (EEG)-based brain-computer interfaces (BCIs) face significant deployment challenges due to inter-subject variability, signal non-stationarity, and computational constraints. While test-time adaptation (TTA) mitigates distribution shifts under online data streams without per-use calibration sessions, existing TTA approaches heavily rely on explicitly defined loss objectives that require backpropagation for updating model parameters, which incurs computational overhead, privacy risks, and sensitivity to noisy data streams. This paper proposes Backpropagation-Free Transformations (BFT), a TTA approach for EEG decoding that eliminates such issues. BFT applies multiple sample-wise transformations of knowledge-guided augmentations or approximate Bayesian inference to each test trial, generating multiple prediction scores for a single test sample. A learning-to-rank module enhances the weighting of these predictions, enabling robust aggregation for uncertainty suppression during inference under theoretical justifications. Extensive experiments on five EEG datasets of motor imagery classification and driver drowsiness regression tasks demonstrate the effectiveness, versatility, robustness, and efficiency of BFT. This research enables lightweight plug-and-play BCIs on resource-constrained devices, broadening the real-world deployment of decoding algorithms for EEG-based BCI.

7. TIPI: Test Time Adaptation with Transformation Invariance (Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition 2023, cites: 44)
   When deploying a machine learning model to a new environment, we often encounter the distribution shift problem - meaning the target data distribution is different from the model's training distribution. In this paper, we assume that labels are not provided for this new domain, and that we do not store the source data (e.g., for privacy reasons). It has been shown that even small shifts in the data distribution can affect the model's performance severely. Test Time Adaptation offers a means to combat this problem, as it allows the model to adapt during test time to the new data distribution, using only unlabeled test data batches. To achieve this, the predominant approach is to optimize a surrogate loss on the test-time unlabeled target data. In particular, minimizing the prediction's entropy on target samples [34] has received much interest as it is task-agnostic and does not require altering the model's training phase (e.g., does not require adding a self-supervised task during training on the source domain). However, as the target data's batch size is often small in real-world scenarios (e.g., autonomous driving models process each few frames in real-time), we argue that this surrogate loss is not optimal since it often collapses with small batch sizes. To tackle this problem, in this paper, we propose to use an invariance regularizer as the surrogate loss during test-time adaptation, motivated by our theoretical results regarding the model's performance under input transformations. The resulting method (TIPI-Test time adaPtation with transformation Invariance) is validated with extensive experiments in various benchmarks (Cifar10-C, Cifar100-C, ImageNet-C, DIGITS, and VisDA17). Remarkably, TIPI is robust against small batch sizes (as small as 2 in our experiments), and consistently outperforms TENT [34] in all settings. Our code is released at https://github.com/atuannguyen/TIPI.

10. TTVD: Towards a Geometric Framework for Test-Time Adaptation Based on Voronoi Diagram (International Conference on Learning Representations 2024, cites: 2)
   Deep learning models often struggle with generalization when deploying on real-world data, due to the common distributional shift to the training data. Test-time adaptation (TTA) is an emerging scheme used at inference time to address this issue. In TTA, models are adapted online at the same time when making predictions to test data. Neighbor-based approaches have gained attention recently, where prototype embeddings provide location information to alleviate the feature shift between training and testing data. However, due to their inherit limitation of simplicity, they often struggle to learn useful patterns and encounter performance degradation. To confront this challenge, we study the TTA problem from a geometric point of view. We first reveal that the underlying structure of neighbor-based methods aligns with the Voronoi Diagram, a classical computational geometry model for space partitioning. Building on this observation, we propose the Test-Time adjustment by Voronoi Diagram guidance (TTVD), a novel framework that leverages the benefits of this geometric property. Specifically, we explore two key structures: 1) Cluster-induced Voronoi Diagram (CIVD): This integrates the joint contribution of self-supervision and entropy-based methods to provide richer information. 2) Power Diagram (PD): A generalized version of the Voronoi Diagram that refines partitions by assigning weights to each Voronoi cell. Our experiments under rigid, peer-reviewed settings on CIFAR-10-C, CIFAR-100-C, ImageNet-C, and ImageNet-R shows that TTVD achieves remarkable improvements compared to state-of-the-art methods. Moreover, extensive experimental results also explore the effects of batch size and class imbalance, which are two scenarios commonly encountered in real-world applications. These analyses further validate the robustness and adaptability of our proposed framework.

   While there are many advantages to deploying machine learning models on edge devices, the resource constraints of mobile platforms, the dynamic nature of the environment, and differences between the distribution of training versus in-the-wild data make such deployments challenging. Our approach minimizes computational costs by dynamically updating normalization statistics without backpropagation, which frees LeanTTA from the common pitfall of relying on large batches and historical data, making our method robust to realistic deployment scenarios. We validate our framework across sensor modalities, demonstrating significant improvements over state-of-the-art TTA methods, including a 15.7% error reduction, peak memory usage of only 11.2MB for ResNet18, and fast adaptation within an order-of-magnitude of normal inference speeds on-device. LeanTTA provides a robust solution for achieving the right trade offs between accuracy and system efficiency in edge deployments, addressing the unique challenges posed by limited data and varied operational conditions.

3. Meta-Learned Adaptive Optimization for Robust Human Mesh Recovery with Uncertainty-Aware Parameter Updates ( 2026, cites: 0)
   Human mesh recovery from single images remains challenging due to inherent depth ambiguity and limited generalization across domains. While recent methods combine regression and optimization approaches, they struggle with poor initialization for test-time refinement and inefficient parameter updates during optimization. Our approach introduces three key innovations: (1) a meta-learning strategy that simulates test-time optimization during training to learn better parameter initializations, (2) a selective parameter caching mechanism that identifies and freezes converged joints to reduce computational overhead, and (3) distribution-based adaptive updates that sample parameter changes from learned distributions, enabling robust exploration while quantifying uncertainty. 4. MetaWriter: Personalized Handwritten Text Recognition Using Meta-Learned Prompt Tuning (Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition 2025, cites: 3)
   Recent advancements in handwritten text recognition (HTR) have enabled the effective conversion of handwritten text to digital formats. However, achieving robust recognition across diverse writing styles remains challenging. Traditional HTR methods lack writer-specific personalization at test time due to limitations in model architecture and training strategies. Existing attempts to bridge this gap, through gradient-based meta-learning, still require labeled examples and suffer from parameter-inefficient fine-tuning, leading to substantial computational and memory overhead. To overcome these challenges, we propose an efficient framework that formulates personalization as prompt tuning, incorporating an auxiliary image reconstruction task with a self-supervised loss to guide prompt adaptation with unlabeled test-time examples. We validate our approach on the RIMES and IAM Handwriting Database benchmarks, where it consistently outperforms previous state-ofthe-art methods while using 20x fewer parameters. We believe this represents a significant advancement in personalized handwritten text recognition, paving the way for more reliable and practical deployment in resource-constrained scenarios.

5. Possibilistic distribution distance metric: a robust domain adaptation learning method (Frontiers in Neuroscience 2023, cites: 3)
   The affective Brain-Computer Interface (aBCI) systems, which achieve predictions for individual subjects through training on multiple subjects, often cannot achieve satisfactory results due to the differences in Electroencephalogram (EEG) patterns between subjects. One tried to use Subject-specific classifiers, but there was a lack of sufficient labeled data. To solve this problem, Domain Adaptation (DA) has recently received widespread attention in the field of EEG-based emotion recognition. Domain adaptation (DA) learning aims to solve the problem of inconsistent distributions between training and test datasets and has received extensive attention. Most existing methods use Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) or its variants to minimize the problem of domain distribution inconsistency. However, noisy data in the domain can lead to significant drift in domain means, which can affect the adaptability performance of learning methods based on MMD and its variants to some extent. Therefore, we propose a robust domain adaptation learning method with possibilistic distribution distance measure. Firstly, the traditional MMD criterion is transformed into a novel possibilistic clustering model to weaken the influence of noisy data, thereby constructing a robust possibilistic distribution distance metric (P-DDM) criterion. Then the robust effectiveness of domain distribution alignment is further improved by a fuzzy entropy regularization term. The proposed P-DDM is in theory proved which be an upper bound of the traditional distribution distance measure method MMD criterion under certain conditions. Therefore, minimizing P-DDM can effectively optimize the MMD objective. Secondly, based on the P-DDM criterion, a robust domain adaptation classifier based on P-DDM (C-PDDM) is proposed, which adopts the Laplacian matrix to preserve the geometric consistency of instances in the source domain and target domain for improving the label propagation performance. At the same time, by maximizing the use of source domain discriminative information to minimize domain discrimination error, the generalization performance of the learning model is further improved. Finally, a large number of experiments and analyses on multiple EEG datasets (i.e., SEED and SEED-IV) show that the proposed method has superior or comparable robustness performance (i.e., has increased by around 10%) in most cases.

6. Enhancing Semi-Supervised Learning With Concept Drift Detection and Self-Training: A Study on Classifier Diversity and Performance (IEEE Access 2025, cites: 2)
   Machine learning algorithms that assist in decision-making are becoming crucial in several areas, such as healthcare, finance, marketing, etc. Algorithms exposed to a larger and more relevant amount of training data tend to perform better. However, the availability of labeled data without human expert intervention is a challenging task, especially in data stream learning with concept drifts, where data is generated rapidly, in real-time, with the possibility of changes in the data distribution. Concept drift occurs in supervised, semi-supervised and unsupervised learning environments, and is addressed through different approaches such as statistics, machine learning, among others. Currently, the use of drift detectors with base classifiers in semi-supervised learning is uncommon. Semi-supervised classifiers often consume a lot of memory and run-time, and the addition of a detection mechanism increases the computational cost. Furthermore, classification in semi-supervised environments can lead to problems related to labeling data to training: an error in this process can negatively impact model performance. This article investigates the use of supervised concept drift detectors in semi-supervised learning problems, highlighting how the detectors can improve the performance of classification. It also explores the influence of diversity in classifier ensembles, showing that increased diversity contributes to enhanced accuracy and robustness of models in concept drift scenarios. Additionally, it introduces a self-training approach to provide more labels and optimize model learning and adaptation. This research also details updates in the Massive Online Analysis (MOA) framework, that supports the simulation of semi-supervised scenarios. The experiments conducted to test the proposed approach used Hoeffding Tree (HT) and Naive Bayes (NB) as base classifiers, which were also employed as members of the ensembles used in this research. These classifiers were combined with several detectors and tested on a total of 84 artificial and five real-world datasets. The experiments were conducted with 15% and 30% of labeled data, the main percentages addressed in this research, while 100% was used to provide additional grounding in some cases. The results indicate that detectors created for supervised learning can be effectively used in semi-supervised environments. Furthermore, the tests using the introduced self-training approach demonstrates that the inclusion of additional labels significantly improves the performance of the classifiers. These findings may lead to a paradigm shift for future research, as many researchers have not considered concept drift detectors as a viable alternative due to the limited presence of labels in most real-world data streams.

7. Meta-Learning Related Tasks with Recurrent Networks: Optimization and Generalization (IEEE International Joint Conference on Neural Network 2018, cites: 2)
   There have been recent interest in meta-learning systems: i.e., networks that are trained to learn across multiple tasks. This paper focuses on optimization and generalization of a meta-learning system based on recurrent networks. The optimization investigates the influence of diverse structures and parameters on its performance. We demonstrate the generalization (robustness) of our meta-learning system to learn across multiple tasks including tasks unseen during the metatraining phase. We introduce a meta-cost function (Mean Squared Fair Error) that enhances the performance of the system by not penalizing it during transitions to learning a new task. Evaluation results are presented for Boolean and quadratic functions datasets. The best performance is obtained using a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) topology without a forget gate and with a clipped memory cell. The results demonstrate i) the impact of different LSTM architectures, parameters, and error functions on the meta-learning process; ii) that the mean squared fair error function does improve performance for best learning; and iii) the robustness of our meta-learning framework as it generalizes well when tested on tasks unseen during meta-training. Comparison between No-Forget-Gate LSTM and Gated Recurrent Unit also suggest that absence of a memory cell tends to degrade performance.

8. Long short-term memory (LSTM) networks for precision prediction of Schottky barrier photodiode behavior at different illumination levels (Scientific Reports 2025, cites: 3)
   This study has focused on modeling and predicting the electrical properties and parameters of CdZnO interlayered Al/p-Si Schottky Diodes (SDs) using the Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) algorithm. The primary aim of this study was to develop a robust predictive model that accurately captures how dopant concentration and illumination levels influence the electrical behavior of SDs. Using the temporal gating and memory capabilities of the LSTM architecture, we proposed a time- and cost-efficient alternative deep-learning model to extensive experimental procedures, ensuring that the diode characterization process could be accelerated without compromising accuracy. The dataset comprises a combination of three Al/CdZnO/p-Si SDs containing different Cd dopant ratios (10%, 20%, and 30%) and five different levels of illumination (50, 100, 150, 200, and 250 mW/cm2). Predictions for electrical parameters, including ideality factor (n), barrier height (FB), and series resistance (Rs), were conducted using the traditional I–V method, Cheung’s analysis, and Norde’s method. To evaluate the LSTM model predictions, one diode at a specific illumination level was selected as the test set. At the same time, the remaining dataset was divided into 80% for training and 20% for validation. The optimization algorithm was selected as Adaptive Moment Estimation (Adam), and the root mean squared error (RMSE) served as the loss function. Hyperparameters, including the number of epochs (150) and batch size (64), were determined empirically to balance computational efficiency and model performance. Results indicate that predictions on Diode 1 demonstrate strong performance at 50, 100, and 150 mW/cm2 illuminations, exhibiting RMSE values of 11.5, 7.2, and 11 mA, respectively, and R² values exceeding 0.98. LSTM shows on Diode 2 consistently lower errors, achieving a minimum RMSE of 6.22 mA at 100 W (R²=0.993). Diode 3 predictions elevated RMSE and mean absolute error (MAE) at both 50 and 250 mW/cm2. Across Traditional I–V, Cheung’s, and Norde’s analyses, the LSTM model yields close agreement with experimental measurements, particularly for barrier height and ideality factor. In conclusion, the LSTM model offers a reliable, cost-effective, and time-efficient alternative to exhaustive Schottky diodes experimental measurements. By accurately capturing the nonlinear interplay of doping level and illumination in SDs, this method provides a practical way to expedite device characterization. These findings highlight the potential of data-driven deep learning approaches in semiconductor research and open avenues for broader applications of LSTM architectures in predicting electronic and optoelectronic device parameters.

9. Deep Q-learning policy optimization method for enhancing generalization in autonomous vehicle control (Information, Computing and Intelligent systems 2025, cites: 0)
   The development of autonomous vehicle control policies based on deep reinforcement learning is a principal technical problem for cyber-physical systems, fundamentally constrained by the high dimensionality of state spaces, inherent algorithmic instability, and a pervasive risk of policy over-specialization that severely limits generalization to real-world scenarios. The object of this investigation is the iterative process of forming a robust control policy within a simulated environment, while the subject focuses on the influence of specialized reward structures and initial training conditions on policy convergence and generalization capability. The study's aim is to develop and empirically evaluate a deep Q-learning policy optimization method that utilizes dynamic initial conditions to mitigate over-specialization and achieve stable, globally optimal adaptive control. The developed method formalizes two optimization criteria. First, the adaptive reward function serves as the safety and convergence criterion, defined hierarchically with major penalties for collision, intermediate incentives for passing checkpoints and a continuous minor penalty for elapsed time to drive efficiency. Second, the mechanism of dynamic initial conditions acts as the policy generalization criterion, designed to inject necessary stochasticity into the state distribution. The agent is modeled as a vehicle equipped with an eight-sensor system providing 360 degrees coverage, making decisions from a discrete action space of seven options. Its ten-dimensional state vector integrates normalized sensor distance readings with normalized dynamic characteristics, including speed and angular error. Empirical testing confirmed the policy's vulnerability under baseline fixed-start conditions, where the agent demonstrated over-specialization and stagnated at a traveled distance of approximately 960 conventional units after 40,000 episodes. The subsequent application of the dynamic initial conditions criterion successfully addressed this failure. By forcing the agent to rely on its generalized state mapping instead of trajectory memory, this approach successfully overcame the learning plateau, enabling the agent to achieve full, collision-free track traversal between 53,000 and 54,000 episodes. Final optimization, driven by penalty, reduced the total track completion time by nearly half. This verification confirms the method's value in producing robust, stable, and efficient control policies suitable for integration into autonomous transport cyber-physical systems.

10. Newly‐acquired words are more phonologically robust in verbal short‐term memory when they have associated semantic representations (Neuropsychologia 2017, cites: 23)
   <no abstract>

1. Online Reasoning Calibration: Test-Time Training Enables Generalizable Conformal LLM Reasoning ( 2026, cites: 0)
   While test-time scaling has enabled large language models to solve highly difficult tasks, state-of-the-art results come at exorbitant compute costs. These inefficiencies can be attributed to the miscalibration of post-trained language models, and the lack of calibration in popular sampling techniques. Here, we present Online Reasoning Calibration (ORCA), a framework for calibrating the sampling process that draws upon conformal prediction and test-time training. Specifically, we introduce a meta-learning procedure that updates the calibration module for each input. This allows us to provide valid confidence estimates under distributional shift, e.g. in thought patterns that occur across different stages of reasoning, or in prompt distributions between model development and deployment. ORCA not only provides theoretical guarantees on conformal risks, but also empirically shows higher efficiency and generalization across different reasoning tasks. At risk level $\delta=0.1$, ORCA improves Qwen2.5-32B efficiency on in-distribution tasks with savings up to 47.5% with supervised labels and 40.7% with self-consistency labels. Under zero-shot out-of-domain settings, it improves MATH-500 savings from 24.8% of the static calibration baseline to 67.0% while maintaining a low empirical error rate, and the same trend holds across model families and downstream benchmarks. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/wzekai99/ORCA.

messages.content:
You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
  Score 2: token pruning with a different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of known pruning method.

### Block B — "compositional reasoning with discrete latent structure"
  Score 5: discrete latent composition graphs induced via learned
           merge policy + RL reward shaped by downstream task.
  Score 4: VQ-VAE codes as discrete symbols + neural composition.
  Score 3: ST Gumbel-Softmax for discrete tree induction.
  Score 2: VQ-VAE with renamed quantization function.
  Score 1: standard transformer over text.

### Block C — "time-series anomaly detection in irregular sequences"
  Score 5: time-aware transformer with continuous-time positional
           encoding + downstream-cost-tuned anomaly threshold via
           RL on alarm precision/recall trade-off.
  Score 4: Neural CDE encoder + density-ratio anomaly score —
           combination not surfaced as joint application.
  Score 3: ODE-RNN encoder with reconstruction-error scoring —
           established pattern.
  Score 2: GRU autoencoder on resampled regular intervals with a
           different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of an LSTM forecasting + z-score
           baseline.

## ReAct loop — output format each round (no markdown)

Each round, output EXACTLY ONE of these two formats:

Search format (issue when more evidence is needed):
    Thought: <1-3 sentences on which mechanism pairing you want to
              verify in prior work>
    Query: <semantic scholar search keywords>

Finalize format (issue ONLY when ready to score):
    Reasoning: <2-4 sentences synthesizing what searches found and
                how it maps to a score anchor>
    Prior works: <newline-separated list of papers/methods that most
                  directly cover the proposed method, one per line
                  in the form "paper_title or method_name (year): one-line
                  summary of overlap with the proposed method".
                  Use "(none)" if score is 5 with no near-clone surfaced.>
    Score: <integer 1-5>

Hard rules:
  - DO NOT mix the search and finalize formats. If you output a
    `Query:` line, the caller treats this round as a search and
    ignores any `Score:` you write — do not waste tokens on Score
    in a search round.
  - You MUST issue at least one search before finalizing UNLESS the
    method is a trivial restatement of a known technique.
  - When you write the literal token "Score:" inside Reasoning to
    cite an anchor, write it INLINE (e.g., "matches the Score 4
    anchor"), never on its own line — the parser anchors to lines
    starting with "Score:".
  - Prior works lines must each start with "  - " (two spaces, dash,
    space) so the parser can extract them. Do NOT include a trailing
    "Score:" inside a Prior works line.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Test-time distributions diverge from training data along axes
the model never saw; how can we make models robust to such
shifts without observing the target distribution at training?


### Proposed method
We propose Robust-Without-Target (RWT), a two-stage recipe that combines training-time shift-agnostic regularization with a test-time adapter and safety wrapper. At training, we (a) replace deterministic data dropout/reweighting with a probabilistic, influence-bounded sampler that caps the gradient of per-example sampling probabilities; and (b) add a multiresolution auxiliary head to regularize features across granularities. Concretely, we compute per-example influence signals on a held-out split and sample with a linear/sigmoid function whose slope σ bounds sensitivity; this mimics distributionally robust training within a χ²-ball around the empirical distribution while avoiding overconfidence on the validation mix. In parallel, we train a light auxiliary prediction head at a coarser or alternative resolution (e.g., class clusters, attribute tags, or subword/character analogs), applying a weighted multi-task loss to stabilize semantics across unseen axes.

Next, we meta-learn a test-time adaptation operator across synthetic shifts, without access to the true target distribution. We define a set of plausible shift generators (e.g., style/texture perturbations, resolution changes, occlusion/masking, label-imbalance resampling) and construct inner–outer loops: an inner loop performs a few test-time steps using unsupervised/self-supervised objectives (entropy minimization, consistency across augmentations, and agreement between main and auxiliary heads); the outer loop optimizes the base model so that post-adaptation performance on held-out views improves. This yields a MAML-like adapter that can quickly specialize to novel conditions using only the incoming test stream.

At inference, we attach a training-free, non-parametric error-compensation memory. The system stores a rolling buffer of recent test representations, base predictions, and any available outcomes; when labels are unavailable or delayed, it stores proxy nonconformity scores (e.g., softmax margin, disagreement across augmentations/heads). For each new input, we retrieve similar states by attention over the buffer and estimate a residual bias to add to the base prediction, with a temperature and clamp to keep corrections bounded. The buffer is refreshed online, enabling self-correction even under rapid drift.

Finally, we wrap predictions with an online-calibrated conformal safety layer. We score each output with a monotone, model-derived nonconformity (e.g., 1 − max softmax or margin) and maintain a threshold κ updated with a decaying-step-size rule to target 1−α coverage. Below κ we return a point prediction; above κ we either return a small set (top-k covering set) or abstain, depending on the application. This provides coverage guarantees and graceful degradation under unforeseen shifts. The full system requires no target-domain samples at training: robustness comes from influence-bounded sampling and multiresolution regularization, quick specialization is delivered by the meta-learned adapter, rapid local bias correction comes from the error memory, and reliability is enforced by online conformal calibration.

### Rationale
Unknown, out-of-support shifts can break models along axes unseen in training. RWT addresses this in layered fashion: (1) bounded-gradient probabilistic sampling reduces the model’s sensitivity to any one training mix and improves worst-case risk within a principled uncertainty set, while multiresolution multitask losses pull features toward semantics that persist across scales, reducing overfit to spurious axes; (2) a meta-learned adapter trains the model to improve after a small, unsupervised adaptation step on synthetic, diverse perturbations, enabling quick specialization at deployment without target-domain pre-exposure; (3) a non-parametric error memory adds fast, training-free local corrections that follow the current stream, even with weak or delayed supervision; and (4) conformal calibration converts raw scores into coverage-controlled decisions, ensuring reliability despite residual mismatch.

Because no single mechanism fully protects against unseen axes, the combination is key: influence-bounded training and multiresolution regularization make the base model broadly stable; the adapter and memory handle rapid or structural drifts at test time; and conformal thresholds bound risk when uncertainty spikes. Crucially, none of these components require observing the true target distribution during training.

### Inspired seeds (the ideation's input retrieval)
1. [IR_REC] Less Is Better: Unweighted Data Subsampling via Influence Function (1912.01321)
   problem: Influence-based deterministic ‘dropout’ sampling can become over-confident on a specific validation distribution, hurting out-of-sample generalization under distribution shift.
   method:  Develop probabilistic sampling functions with bounded gradients to control confidence and improve robustness across distributions within a χ²-divergence ball around the empirical distribution. Theoretically, they prove the worst-case risk over this uncertainty set is Lipschitz in the influence vector with constant scaling as O(σ√((2δ+1)/n)), where σ bounds the sampling function’s gradient (Theorem 3), highlighting instability of deterministic dropout (unbounded gradient). They also introduce a surrogate confidence metric Γ(φ)=‖θ_ε−θ‖² and show it shares the same σ-dependent change rate (Theorem 4), and instantiate practical Linear and Sigmoid sampling functions that tune σ via a single parameter.

2. [LLM_NLP] On Training Data Influence of GPT Models (2404.07840)
   problem: Training data attribution (TDA) for autoregressive GPT models has focused mostly on test loss and often ties simulator parameters to seen training indices, limiting generalization to unseen data and overlooking richer training dynamics.
   method:  GPTfluence introduces a featurized simulator that models training as an n-th order Markov process with multiplicative and additive influence factors. It computes per-batch effects via α and β, which are aggregated from per-example factors A_i,j and B_i derived from pairwise interactions between frozen pre-trained text encodings of training and test examples. Using a pre-trained encoder (e.g., BERT/GPT) for both training and test inputs enables parameter sharing over features rather than indices, allowing autoregressive prediction of full performance trajectories and robust generalization to unseen training/test data.

3. [IR_REC] ReLoop2: Building Self-Adaptive Recommendation Models via Responsive Error Compensation Loop (2306.08808)
   problem: Online recommender systems face non-stationary data and test-time distribution shift, while gradient-based incremental updates adapt too slowly for large CTR models.
   method:  ReLoop2 introduces a slow–fast learning paradigm with a training-free, non-parametric error memory that stores recent error samples (hidden state, base prediction, and ground-truth) observed during serving. At inference, it retrieves similar samples to the current hidden representation and uses attention with a temperature to estimate the prediction error; this error is then used to compensate the base model’s output with a tunable weight and clamping to [0,1]. The approach continually refreshes the memory online, creating a self-correcting loop for fast test-time adaptation, and is model-agnostic and orthogonal to incremental training.

4. [MULTIMODAL] Plug-and-Play VQA: Zero-shot VQA by Conjoining Large Pretrained Models with Zero Training (2210.08773)
   problem: Zero-shot VQA needs to reason over images and questions without any additional multimodal training; generic, image-wide captions often miss question-relevant details, limiting a text-only QA model’s effectiveness.
   method:  Use a pretrained VLM (BLIP) both to localize question-relevant regions and to generate diverse, question-guided captions. Relevance is computed by a GradCAM-style aggregation over BLIP’s cross-attention in the Image-grounded Text Encoder: multiply cross-attention scores by gradients of the image–text similarity and average over heads/tokens to get per-patch relevance, then sample K′ patches per caption proportionally to this relevance. From these patches, generate N diverse captions via stochastic top-k decoding (e.g., k=50) and drop near-duplicates. This produces captions with higher answer-hit rates and boosts zero-shot VQA accuracy, outperforming generic/random patch captioning and even 5 human captions when using 100 question-guided captions.

5. [MULTIMODAL] Visual Semantic Reasoning for Image-Text Matching (1909.02701)
   problem: Pure metric learning may not sufficiently enforce that the learned image embeddings encode captionable semantics needed for robust cross-modal alignment.
   method:  Jointly optimize a hinge-based triplet ranking loss with hard negatives in the shared embedding space (using simple inner-product similarity) together with an auxiliary sequence-to-sequence caption generation loss with attention on the same visual features. This multi-objective training encourages embeddings that are both discriminative for retrieval and predictive of captions, improving R@1 over training without the generation objective.

6. [MULTIMODAL] DetCLIPv2: Scalable Open-Vocabulary Object Detection Pre-training via Word-Region Alignment (2304.04514)
   problem: Open-vocabulary detection needs to learn from massive web image–text pairs, but captions lack instance-level boxes, VL distillation suffers domain gaps, and pseudo-labeling limits accuracy and scalability.
   method:  DetCLIPv2 performs end-to-end joint pre-training on detection, grounding, and raw image–text pairs under a unified formulation, learning fine-grained word–region alignment directly from captions. For each noun phrase, it matches the best region proposal via a max-similarity (1-to-1) set alignment and optimizes a text-to-image contrastive loss only, which mitigates the partial-label problem in web data. It selects proposals for contrast by scoring regions with the maximum similarity to all concepts in the local batch, focusing on text-relevant regions. Alternating data types during training creates a flywheel between localization and recognition, and using low-resolution inputs for web pairs enables efficient scaling (e.g., 13× more pairs with similar training time).

7. [CV] Robust Instance Segmentation through Reasoning about Multi-Object Occlusion (2012.02107)
   problem: Per-object, per-pixel feed-forward segmentations often conflict in overlap regions (multiple objects claiming the same pixels), especially under heavy or same-category occlusions, leading to erroneous masks and classifications.
   method:  Introduce an Occlusion Reasoning Module (ORM) that operates on the per-object likelihood maps. It detects conflicts where two objects both predict foreground, resolves them via pixel-level competition by assigning each contested pixel to the object with higher foreground likelihood, and then recovers the pairwise occlusion order by comparing the number of reassigned pixels in the conflict set. Using this order, it enforces consistent labeling in overlaps and feeds the corrected occlusion priors back into the CompositionalNet to mask occluded features and recompute class likelihoods, iterating this top-down self-correction to improve both segmentation and classification under occlusion.

8. [CV] S2S2: Semantic Stacking for Robust Semantic Segmentation in Medical Imaging (2412.13156)
   problem: Medical image segmentation models trained on scarce, low-diversity data often learn biased, noisy semantic features, hurting both in-domain accuracy and out-of-domain robustness. Existing fixes (specialized augmentations/architectures) rely on domain assumptions that may not generalize across modalities (CT, MRI, RGB).
   method:  Introduce Semantic Stacking (S2S2), an add-on, domain-agnostic training strategy that estimates a denoised semantic representation by stacking images that share the same ground-truth segmentation map and guiding the network toward that representation. Practically, S2S2 adds a semantic consistency loss between features from two images per mask sampled from the stack and combines it with the standard segmentation loss. The consistency loss is applied to both encoder and decoder features and instantiated as 1 − cosine similarity, improving feature robustness without changing the base architecture or augmentation pipeline. (Paper: Sec. 1, 3; Fig. 2; lines 29–31, 55–74, 89–95, 123–126; Code: TransUNet trainer.py utils.ConsistencyLoss)

9. [CV] Low-Resource Vision Challenges for Foundation Models (2401.04716)
   problem: Foundation models tokenize images into large patches, missing the subtle, fine-grained local details that distinguish classes in specialized low-resource domains.
   method:  Without retraining the backbone, split the pre-trained ViT patch-embedding linear projection kernel K (e.g., q=14) into smaller sub-kernels (u=7, T=49) and apply them to subregions of each original patch to capture fine-grained local patterns. Learn a lightweight weighting vector to fuse sub-kernel feature maps into a patch-level feature, then max-pool and flatten so the token dimensionality aligns with the frozen foundation model. This parameter-efficient tokenization enhances attention to small local cues and outperforms state-of-the-art fine-grained methods under severe data scarcity.

10. [ROBOTICS] Meta Reinforcement Learning for Sim-to-real Domain Adaptation (1909.12906)
   problem: Policies trained in simulation often fail to transfer to real robots under unknown or varying dynamics; domain-randomized policies can adapt unpredictably and even deteriorate during on-policy updates on hardware.
   method:  Use a gradient-based meta-reinforcement learning framework (MAML-like) that trains a meta-policy across tasks defined by randomized dynamics, explicitly optimizing expected return after N inner-loop adaptation steps. At deployment, the policy performs K-rollout inner-loop updates with vanilla policy gradient to quickly adapt to the current dynamics, while the outer meta-update backpropagates through the inner steps and is optimized with PPO. Experiments in simulation and on a KUKA puck-hitting task show stable, consistent adaptation and better performance than a domain-randomization baseline.

11. [RL] Speeding up Policy Simulation in Supply Chain RL (2406.01939)
   problem: In supply chain RL (and many long-horizon MDPs), simulating a single trajectory under a state-dependent policy is a bottleneck because policy evaluations are inherently serial and expensive (often DNN-based), while standard parallel discrete event methods like Time Warp suffer rollbacks and GPU-unfriendly message passing.
   method:  Introduce Picard iteration for policy simulation: split the horizon [T] across M (virtual) processes and initialize a cache of actions for all time-steps. 1. Test-Time Adaptation with Perturbation Consistency Learning (arXiv.org 2023, cites: 1)
   Currently, pre-trained language models (PLMs) do not cope well with the distribution shift problem, resulting in models trained on the training set failing in real test scenarios. To address this problem, the test-time adaptation (TTA) shows great potential, which updates model parameters to suit the test data at the testing time. Existing TTA methods rely on well-designed auxiliary tasks or self-training strategies based on pseudo-label. However, these methods do not achieve good trade-offs regarding performance gains and computational costs. To obtain some insights into such a dilemma, we take two representative TTA methods, i.e., Tent and OIL, for exploration and find that stable prediction is the key to achieving a good balance. Accordingly, in this paper, we propose perturbation consistency learning (PCL), a simple test-time adaptation method to promote the model to make stable predictions for samples with distribution shifts. Extensive experiments on adversarial robustness and cross-lingual transferring demonstrate that our method can achieve higher or comparable performance with less inference time over strong PLM backbones and previous state-of-the-art TTA methods.

2. FRET: Feature Redundancy Elimination for Test Time Adaptation (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 2)
   Test-Time Adaptation (TTA) aims to enhance the generalization of deep learning models when faced with test data that exhibits distribution shifts from the training data. In this context, only a pre-trained model and unlabeled test data are available, making it particularly relevant for privacy-sensitive applications. In practice, we observe that feature redundancy in embeddings tends to increase as domain shifts intensify in TTA. However, existing TTA methods often overlook this redundancy, which can hinder the model's adaptability to new data. To address this issue, we introduce Feature Redundancy Elimination for Test-time Adaptation (FRET), a novel perspective for TTA. A straightforward approach (S-FRET) is to directly minimize the feature redundancy score as an optimization objective to improve adaptation. Despite its simplicity and effectiveness, S-FRET struggles with label shifts, limiting its robustness in real-world scenarios. To mitigate this limitation, we further propose Graph-based FRET (G-FRET), which integrates a Graph Convolutional Network (GCN) with contrastive learning. This design not only reduces feature redundancy but also enhances feature discriminability in both the representation and prediction layers. Extensive experiments across multiple model architectures, tasks, and datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of S-FRET and show that G-FRET achieves state-of-the-art performance. Further analysis reveals that G-FRET enables the model to extract non-redundant and highly discriminative features during inference, thereby facilitating more robust test-time adaptation.

   Test-time adaptation (TTA) in federated learning (FL) is crucial for handling unseen data distributions across clients, particularly when faced with domain shifts and skewed class distributions. Although prior work has addressed CI during training through reliable aggregation and local class distribution alignment, these methods typically rely on access to labeled data or coordination among clients, and none address class unsupervised adaptation to dynamic domains or distribution shifts at inference time under federated CI constraints. It addresses both distribution shifts and class imbalance through balanced feature normalization and domain-aware collaboration, without requiring any labeled or raw data from clients.    Test-Time Adaptation (TTA) enables pre-trained models to adjust to distribution shift by learning from unlabeled test-time streams.    When deploying a machine learning model to a new environment, we often encounter the distribution shift problem - meaning the target data distribution is different from the model's training distribution. In this paper, we assume that labels are not provided for this new domain, and that we do not store the source data (e.g., for privacy reasons). It has been shown that even small shifts in the data distribution can affect the model's performance severely. Test Time Adaptation offers a means to combat this problem, as it allows the model to adapt during test time to the new data distribution, using only unlabeled test data batches. To achieve this, the predominant approach is to optimize a surrogate loss on the test-time unlabeled target data. In particular, minimizing the prediction's entropy on target samples [34] has received much interest as it is task-agnostic and does not require altering the model's training phase (e.g., does not require adding a self-supervised task during training on the source domain). However, as the target data's batch size is often small in real-world scenarios (e.g., autonomous driving models process each few frames in real-time), we argue that this surrogate loss is not optimal since it often collapses with small batch sizes. To tackle this problem, in this paper, we propose to use an invariance regularizer as the surrogate loss during test-time adaptation, motivated by our theoretical results regarding the model's performance under input transformations. The resulting method (TIPI-Test time adaPtation with transformation Invariance) is validated with extensive experiments in various benchmarks (Cifar10-C, Cifar100-C, ImageNet-C, DIGITS, and VisDA17). Remarkably, TIPI is robust against small batch sizes (as small as 2 in our experiments), and consistently outperforms TENT [34] in all settings. Our code is released at https://github.com/atuannguyen/TIPI.

   Deep learning models often struggle with generalization when deploying on real-world data, due to the common distributional shift to the training data. Test-time adaptation (TTA) is an emerging scheme used at inference time to address this issue. In TTA, models are adapted online at the same time when making predictions to test data. Neighbor-based approaches have gained attention recently, where prototype embeddings provide location information to alleviate the feature shift between training and testing data. However, due to their inherit limitation of simplicity, they often struggle to learn useful patterns and encounter performance degradation. To confront this challenge, we study the TTA problem from a geometric point of view. We first reveal that the underlying structure of neighbor-based methods aligns with the Voronoi Diagram, a classical computational geometry model for space partitioning. Building on this observation, we propose the Test-Time adjustment by Voronoi Diagram guidance (TTVD), a novel framework that leverages the benefits of this geometric property. Specifically, we explore two key structures: 1) Cluster-induced Voronoi Diagram (CIVD): This integrates the joint contribution of self-supervision and entropy-based methods to provide richer information. 2) Power Diagram (PD): A generalized version of the Voronoi Diagram that refines partitions by assigning weights to each Voronoi cell. Our experiments under rigid, peer-reviewed settings on CIFAR-10-C, CIFAR-100-C, ImageNet-C, and ImageNet-R shows that TTVD achieves remarkable improvements compared to state-of-the-art methods. Moreover, extensive experimental results also explore the effects of batch size and class imbalance, which are two scenarios commonly encountered in real-world applications. These analyses further validate the robustness and adaptability of our proposed framework.

5. Possibilistic distribution distance metric: a robust domain adaptation learning method (Frontiers in Neuroscience 2023, cites: 3)
   The affective Brain-Computer Interface (aBCI) systems, which achieve predictions for individual subjects through training on multiple subjects, often cannot achieve satisfactory results due to the differences in Electroencephalogram (EEG) patterns between subjects. One tried to use Subject-specific classifiers, but there was a lack of sufficient labeled data. To solve this problem, Domain Adaptation (DA) has recently received widespread attention in the field of EEG-based emotion recognition. Domain adaptation (DA) learning aims to solve the problem of inconsistent distributions between training and test datasets and has received extensive attention. Most existing methods use Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) or its variants to minimize the problem of domain distribution inconsistency. However, noisy data in the domain can lead to significant drift in domain means, which can affect the adaptability performance of learning methods based on MMD and its variants to some extent. Therefore, we propose a robust domain adaptation learning method with possibilistic distribution distance measure. Firstly, the traditional MMD criterion is transformed into a novel possibilistic clustering model to weaken the influence of noisy data, thereby constructing a robust possibilistic distribution distance metric (P-DDM) criterion. Then the robust effectiveness of domain distribution alignment is further improved by a fuzzy entropy regularization term. The proposed P-DDM is in theory proved which be an upper bound of the traditional distribution distance measure method MMD criterion under certain conditions. Therefore, minimizing P-DDM can effectively optimize the MMD objective. Secondly, based on the P-DDM criterion, a robust domain adaptation classifier based on P-DDM (C-PDDM) is proposed, which adopts the Laplacian matrix to preserve the geometric consistency of instances in the source domain and target domain for improving the label propagation performance. At the same time, by maximizing the use of source domain discriminative information to minimize domain discrimination error, the generalization performance of the learning model is further improved. Finally, a large number of experiments and analyses on multiple EEG datasets (i.e., SEED and SEED-IV) show that the proposed method has superior or comparable robustness performance (i.e., has increased by around 10%) in most cases.

6. Enhancing Semi-Supervised Learning With Concept Drift Detection and Self-Training: A Study on Classifier Diversity and Performance (IEEE Access 2025, cites: 2)
   Machine learning algorithms that assist in decision-making are becoming crucial in several areas, such as healthcare, finance, marketing, etc. Algorithms exposed to a larger and more relevant amount of training data tend to perform better. However, the availability of labeled data without human expert intervention is a challenging task, especially in data stream learning with concept drifts, where data is generated rapidly, in real-time, with the possibility of changes in the data distribution. Concept drift occurs in supervised, semi-supervised and unsupervised learning environments, and is addressed through different approaches such as statistics, machine learning, among others. Currently, the use of drift detectors with base classifiers in semi-supervised learning is uncommon. Semi-supervised classifiers often consume a lot of memory and run-time, and the addition of a detection mechanism increases the computational cost. Furthermore, classification in semi-supervised environments can lead to problems related to labeling data to training: an error in this process can negatively impact model performance. This article investigates the use of supervised concept drift detectors in semi-supervised learning problems, highlighting how the detectors can improve the performance of classification. It also explores the influence of diversity in classifier ensembles, showing that increased diversity contributes to enhanced accuracy and robustness of models in concept drift scenarios. Additionally, it introduces a self-training approach to provide more labels and optimize model learning and adaptation. This research also details updates in the Massive Online Analysis (MOA) framework, that supports the simulation of semi-supervised scenarios. The experiments conducted to test the proposed approach used Hoeffding Tree (HT) and Naive Bayes (NB) as base classifiers, which were also employed as members of the ensembles used in this research. These classifiers were combined with several detectors and tested on a total of 84 artificial and five real-world datasets. The experiments were conducted with 15% and 30% of labeled data, the main percentages addressed in this research, while 100% was used to provide additional grounding in some cases. The results indicate that detectors created for supervised learning can be effectively used in semi-supervised environments. Furthermore, the tests using the introduced self-training approach demonstrates that the inclusion of additional labels significantly improves the performance of the classifiers. These findings may lead to a paradigm shift for future research, as many researchers have not considered concept drift detectors as a viable alternative due to the limited presence of labels in most real-world data streams.

The optimization investigates the influence of diverse structures and parameters on its performance. We demonstrate the generalization (robustness) of our meta-learning system to learn across multiple tasks including tasks unseen during the metatraining phase. We introduce a meta-cost function (Mean Squared Fair Error) that enhances the performance of the system by not penalizing it during transitions to learning a new task. Evaluation results are presented for Boolean and quadratic functions datasets. The best performance is obtained using a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) topology without a forget gate and with a clipped memory cell. The results demonstrate i) the impact of different LSTM architectures, parameters, and error functions on the meta-learning process; ii) that the mean squared fair error function does improve performance for best learning; and iii) the robustness of our meta-learning framework as it generalizes well when tested on tasks unseen during meta-training. Comparison between No-Forget-Gate LSTM and Gated Recurrent Unit also suggest that absence of a memory cell tends to degrade performance.

8. Long short-term memory (LSTM) networks for precision prediction of Schottky barrier photodiode behavior at different illumination levels (Scientific Reports 2025, cites: 3)
   This study has focused on modeling and predicting the electrical properties and parameters of CdZnO interlayered Al/p-Si Schottky Diodes (SDs) using the Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) algorithm. The primary aim of this study was to develop a robust predictive model that accurately captures how dopant concentration and illumination levels influence the electrical behavior of SDs. Using the temporal gating and memory capabilities of the LSTM architecture, we proposed a time- and cost-efficient alternative deep-learning model to extensive experimental procedures, ensuring that the diode characterization process could be accelerated without compromising accuracy. The dataset comprises a combination of three Al/CdZnO/p-Si SDs containing different Cd dopant ratios (10%, 20%, and 30%) and five different levels of illumination (50, 100, 150, 200, and 250 mW/cm2). Predictions for electrical parameters, including ideality factor (n), barrier height (FB), and series resistance (Rs), were conducted using the traditional I–V method, Cheung’s analysis, and Norde’s method. To evaluate the LSTM model predictions, one diode at a specific illumination level was selected as the test set. At the same time, the remaining dataset was divided into 80% for training and 20% for validation. The optimization algorithm was selected as Adaptive Moment Estimation (Adam), and the root mean squared error (RMSE) served as the loss function. Hyperparameters, including the number of epochs (150) and batch size (64), were determined empirically to balance computational efficiency and model performance. Results indicate that predictions on Diode 1 demonstrate strong performance at 50, 100, and 150 mW/cm2 illuminations, exhibiting RMSE values of 11.5, 7.2, and 11 mA, respectively, and R² values exceeding 0.98. LSTM shows on Diode 2 consistently lower errors, achieving a minimum RMSE of 6.22 mA at 100 W (R²=0.993). Diode 3 predictions elevated RMSE and mean absolute error (MAE) at both 50 and 250 mW/cm2. Across Traditional I–V, Cheung’s, and Norde’s analyses, the LSTM model yields close agreement with experimental measurements, particularly for barrier height and ideality factor. In conclusion, the LSTM model offers a reliable, cost-effective, and time-efficient alternative to exhaustive Schottky diodes experimental measurements. By accurately capturing the nonlinear interplay of doping level and illumination in SDs, this method provides a practical way to expedite device characterization. These findings highlight the potential of data-driven deep learning approaches in semiconductor research and open avenues for broader applications of LSTM architectures in predicting electronic and optoelectronic device parameters.

9. Deep Q-learning policy optimization method for enhancing generalization in autonomous vehicle control (Information, Computing and Intelligent systems 2025, cites: 0)
   The development of autonomous vehicle control policies based on deep reinforcement learning is a principal technical problem for cyber-physical systems, fundamentally constrained by the high dimensionality of state spaces, inherent algorithmic instability, and a pervasive risk of policy over-specialization that severely limits generalization to real-world scenarios. The object of this investigation is the iterative process of forming a robust control policy within a simulated environment, while the subject focuses on the influence of specialized reward structures and initial training conditions on policy convergence and generalization capability. The study's aim is to develop and empirically evaluate a deep Q-learning policy optimization method that utilizes dynamic initial conditions to mitigate over-specialization and achieve stable, globally optimal adaptive control. The developed method formalizes two optimization criteria. First, the adaptive reward function serves as the safety and convergence criterion, defined hierarchically with major penalties for collision, intermediate incentives for passing checkpoints and a continuous minor penalty for elapsed time to drive efficiency. Second, the mechanism of dynamic initial conditions acts as the policy generalization criterion, designed to inject necessary stochasticity into the state distribution. The agent is modeled as a vehicle equipped with an eight-sensor system providing 360 degrees coverage, making decisions from a discrete action space of seven options. Its ten-dimensional state vector integrates normalized sensor distance readings with normalized dynamic characteristics, including speed and angular error. Empirical testing confirmed the policy's vulnerability under baseline fixed-start conditions, where the agent demonstrated over-specialization and stagnated at a traveled distance of approximately 960 conventional units after 40,000 episodes. The subsequent application of the dynamic initial conditions criterion successfully addressed this failure. By forcing the agent to rely on its generalized state mapping instead of trajectory memory, this approach successfully overcame the learning plateau, enabling the agent to achieve full, collision-free track traversal between 53,000 and 54,000 episodes. Final optimization, driven by penalty, reduced the total track completion time by nearly half. This verification confirms the method's value in producing robust, stable, and efficient control policies suitable for integration into autonomous transport cyber-physical systems.

10. Newly‐acquired words are more phonologically robust in verbal short‐term memory when they have associated semantic representations (Neuropsychologia 2017, cites: 23)
   <no abstract>

   While test-time scaling has enabled large language models to solve highly difficult tasks, state-of-the-art results come at exorbitant compute costs. These inefficiencies can be attributed to the miscalibration of post-trained language models, and the lack of calibration in popular sampling techniques. Here, we present Online Reasoning Calibration (ORCA), a framework for calibrating the sampling process that draws upon conformal prediction and test-time training. Specifically, we introduce a meta-learning procedure that updates the calibration module for each input. This allows us to provide valid confidence estimates under distributional shift, e.g. in thought patterns that occur across different stages of reasoning, or in prompt distributions between model development and deployment. ORCA not only provides theoretical guarantees on conformal risks, but also empirically shows higher efficiency and generalization across different reasoning tasks. At risk level $\delta=0.1$, ORCA improves Qwen2.5-32B efficiency on in-distribution tasks with savings up to 47.5% with supervised labels and 40.7% with self-consistency labels. Under zero-shot out-of-domain settings, it improves MATH-500 savings from 24.8% of the static calibration baseline to 67.0% while maintaining a low empirical error rate, and the same trend holds across model families and downstream benchmarks. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/wzekai99/ORCA.
